data.history: Based on the limited research provided, there is no direct information about the Al Bawardi family's history, origins, or development. The only reference to 'Bawardi' appears in the context of Khalid Saud Abd Al Rahman Al Bawardi, a Saudi national born in 1977 who was held at Guantanamo Bay detention camp. Additionally, there is a mention of Mariam Bawardi Kindergarten and Miriam Bawardi Elementary School in the Mar Elias Educational Institutions in Ibillin, Israel, though the connection between these institutions and the Al Bawardi family name is not established in the research. The research also mentions that Houthis have begun training combat divers on Zuqar and Bawardi islands, though this appears to be a geographical reference rather than a family connection.
data.description: No verified information about the Al Bawardi family's history or origins is available in the provided research.

data.history: The Al Bwardy family's documented history centers around their association with the Spinneys supermarket chain through Ali Albwardy, who founded Al Bwardy Investment based in Dubai. The family's connection to the retail business appears to have developed through their acquisition of the Spinneys brand, which was originally established by British entrepreneur Arthur Rawdon Spinney in 1924 as a railway provision merchant in Palestine. After multiple relocations due to regional conflicts, including moves from Palestine to Baghdad and finally to Dubai in 1961, the Spinneys operation eventually came under the ownership and management of Ali Albwardy's investment company. As of 2011, Ali Albwardy had transformed the business into a premium supermarket retailer operating fifty-six stores across the UAE and Oman, with expansion plans for Saudi Arabia.
data.description: The Al Bwardy family are Emirati business owners known for their ownership of the Spinneys supermarket chain through Ali Albwardy's Dubai-based Al Bwardy Investment company. They operate as premium retailers across the Gulf region.
data.modernStatus: The Al Bwardy family operates as major retail business owners in the UAE through Al Bwardy Investment, which owns and manages the Spinneys supermarket chain with fifty-six stores in the UAE, operations in Oman, and expansion plans for Saudi Arabia as of 2011.

timelineEvents.title: Spinneys Expansion Under Al Bwardy

data.history: The Al Sadr family is a Hashemite Arab clerical Shia family originating from Jabal Amel in Lebanon. They are a branch of the Musawi family, tracing their lineage to Musa Ibn Jaafar, the seventh Shia Imam. The Sadr family is specifically a branch of the Charafeddine (شرف الدين) family from Jabal Amel, which itself descends from the Noureddine family. The family's ancestor, Sayyed Saleh Moussawi Ameli, was documented as a descendant of Musa Al Kadhim through 27 generations. The family migrated from Lebanon to Iraq, where they established themselves as prominent religious scholars and political figures. The Al Sadr family has produced numerous Islamic scholars across Iran, Lebanon, and Iraq, becoming one of the most influential Shia clerical families in the region. Notable early figures include Ismail Sadr (died 1919) and his descendants who played crucial roles in 20th-century Iraqi and Lebanese politics. The family gained particular prominence through figures like Mohammad Baqir al-Sadr, who founded the Islamic Dawa Party and was executed by Saddam Hussein in 1980, and Muqtada al-Sadr, who became a major political and military leader in post-2003 Iraq.
data.description: A prominent Hashemite Arab Shia clerical family from Jabal Amel, Lebanon, descended from the seventh Shia Imam Musa al-Kazim, known for producing influential religious scholars and political leaders across Iraq, Lebanon, and Iran.
data.originStory: The Al Sadr family originates from Jabal Amel in present-day southern Lebanon, descending from the Charafeddine family, which traces its lineage through the Noureddine family to Moussa al-Kazim (the seventh Shi'a Imam) and ultimately to Ali ibn Abi Talib and Fatima Zahra, daughter of Prophet Muhammad.
data.tribalOrigin: Musawi family (descendants of Musa Ibn Jaafar)
data.modernStatus: Major political and religious influence in Iraq through the Sadrist Movement and various militia groups; prominent clerical positions across Iraq, Lebanon, and Iran; significant political representation in Iraqi parliament
data.nameEtymology: Al-Sadr (الصدر) means 'the chest' or 'the front' in Arabic, likely referring to a position of leadership or prominence

data.history: The al-Shahristani family is an Iraqi-Iranian clerical Shia family that settled in Karbala from the village of Shahristan, near Isfahan, in the late 18th century. Their patriarch, Sayyid Muhammad-Mehdi al-Shahristani, was born in Shahristan, Isfahan in 1717 and emigrated to Karbala in 1774, where he studied under prominent scholars and became a marja'. The family rose to prominence in Iraq in the late 19th century, with members notable for being Ayatollahs in Karbala. In more recent times, the family has become more academic and political, with their most notable politician being Iraqi statesman Hussain al-Shahristani, who served as minister of Energy, Foreign Affairs, and Higher Education and Scientific Research, and as deputy to Prime Minister Nouri al-Maliki. The family claims agnatic descent from Muhammad's daughter Fatimah through the seventh Shia Imam, Musa al-Kadhim, making them a Musawi family with the honorific title of Sayyid.
data.description: An Iraqi-Iranian clerical Shia family that migrated from Isfahan to Karbala in 1774, rising to prominence as religious scholars and later producing notable politicians including Deputy Prime Minister Hussain al-Shahristani.
data.originStory: The al-Shahristani family originated from the village of Shahristan, near Isfahan, Iran. Their patriarch Sayyid Muhammad-Mehdi al-Shahristani was born there in 1717 before migrating to Karbala in 1774. An ancestor, Sayyid Jalal al-Din Muhammad (known as amir al-nitham), had previously resided in Karbala in the 16th century but migrated to Iran, making Muhammad-Mehdi's migration a return to Karbala.
data.modernStatus: The family has transitioned from primarily religious scholarship to academic and political roles. Hussain al-Shahristani became a prominent nuclear scientist and politician, serving in key Iraqi government positions including Deputy Prime Minister and multiple ministerial roles.
data.nameEtymology: The family name derives from their origin in Shahristan, a village near Isfahan, Iran.

data.history: The Al Ouda family traces its origins to the Bani Khalid, an aristocratic tribe that established an emirate in the Eastern Arabian Peninsula during the 17th and 18th centuries, controlling territory from Kuwait to the Najd before falling to the Al-Saud Dynasty from the Bani Hanifa. The most prominent modern member of the Al Ouda family is Salman bin Fahd bin Abdullah al-Ouda, born in 1956 in al-Basr near Burayda in Al-Qassim province of central Saudi Arabia. His father, Fahd Al-Ouda, belonged to the Al-Ouda branch of the Bani Khalid. Salman al-Ouda became a prominent Islamic scholar and activist, initially supporting the Saudi government but later becoming a key figure in the 1990s opposition movement, particularly through his leadership role in the Committee for the Defense of Legitimate Rights (CDLR) in 1993. After five years of imprisonment from 1994-1999 for anti-government activities, he emerged as a rehabilitated figure who appeared to support the government until his re-arrest in September 2017 for refusing to tweet support for the Saudi blockade of Qatar. As of 2026, he remains detained in al-Ha'ir Prison, with his trial stalled since July 2021 and his health reportedly deteriorating due to prolonged solitary confinement.
data.description: Al Ouda is a branch of the aristocratic Bani Khalid tribe, most notably represented by Islamic scholar Salman al-Ouda, who has been imprisoned since 2017 for his political positions.
data.originStory: The Al Ouda family is documented as belonging to the Al-Ouda branch of the aristocratic Bani Khalid tribe, which historically controlled an emirate in Eastern Arabia from Kuwait to the Najd during the 17th and 18th centuries before their defeat by the Al-Saud Dynasty.
data.modernStatus: The family's most prominent member, Salman al-Ouda, remains detained without charge since September 2017 in al-Ha'ir Prison, Saudi Arabia, with prosecutors having sought the death penalty in 2018. His case has attracted international attention from human rights organizations.

migrationPath.description: Migration of Al Ouda branch from ancestral Bani Khalid territories to central Saudi Arabia

data.history:
The Usfurids (Arabic: آل عصفور, Al ʿUṣfūr) were an Arab dynasty that gained control of Eastern Arabia, including the islands of Bahrain, in 1253 CE. They were a branch of the Uqaylids who had re-migrated to Arabia after the fall of their rule in Syria. The dynasty was named after its founder, ʿUṣfūr bin Rāshid (عُصْفُور بِن رَاشِد), with the name ʿUṣfūr meaning "small bird" in Arabic. Initially, the Usfurids were allies of the Qarmatians and their successors, the Uyunids, but eventually overthrew the latter and seized power themselves after Uyunid authority had been weakened by the 1235 invasion by the Salghurid Atabeg of Fars.

At the height of their power, the Usfurids controlled a state composed of central and eastern Arabia and even ruled parts of Oman. Their rule lasted approximately 150 years, during which they maintained control over the strategically important Bahrain islands and the surrounding Gulf region. In 1354, Arabian forces from the Usfurids invaded Basra and defeated the Mongols, inflicting heavy losses on them. However, Sheikh Hassan al-Jalairi the Great, who succeeded the Mongols in ruling Iraq, sought help from Prince Fawaz bin Muhanna, leader of the Ta'is Tribe, who subsequently defeated the Usfurids and expelled them from Basra.

After 150 years of rule, the Usfurids were eventually overthrown by the Jarwanids, who were in turn overthrown by the Jabrids - another Uqaylid dynasty. The Usfurid legacy continued through various descendants, including Yusuf al-Bahrani, who was noted as a descendant of the Usfurid dynasty. Their rule represents a significant period in the medieval history of Eastern Arabia and the Gulf region.
data.description: The Usfurids were an Arab dynasty of Uqaylid origin that ruled Eastern Arabia and Bahrain from 1253 to approximately 1400 CE. They overthrew the Uyunids and controlled territories from central Arabia to parts of Oman before being succeeded by the Jarwanids.
data.originStory: The Usfurids were a branch of the Uqaylids who re-migrated to Arabia after the fall of their rule in Syria. They were initially allies of the Qarmatians and their successors, the Uyunids, but took advantage of Uyunid weakness following the 1235 Salghurid invasion to overthrow them and establish their own rule over Eastern Arabia.
data.modernStatus: The dynasty ended around 1400 CE when overthrown by the Jarwanids. Modern descendants exist, including members of the Al Usfur family mentioned in historical sources, with some documented in Jordan, Iran, and other Gulf regions.
data.nameEtymology: The dynasty is named after its founder, ʿUṣfūr bin Rāshid. The name ʿUṣfūr (عصفور) means "small bird" in Arabic.

migrationPath.description: Re-migration from Syria to Arabia after fall of Uqaylid rule

data.history: The Al Muqbil family traces its lineage to Ahmad bin Muqbil Al-Tamimi, who belonged to the Wahabah sub-tribe of the Banu Tamim. This lineage is documented through the genealogy of the prominent Islamic scholar Muhammad ibn Saleh al-Uthaymin, whose full lineage is recorded as Muhammad bin Salih bin Muhammad bin Sulayman bin Abdurrahman bin Uthman bin Abdullah bin Abdurrahmaan bin Ahmad bin Muqbil. The family was established in the town of Unayzah in the Qaseem Region of the Najd Province, where they became well-known for their scholarship in religious affairs. The Al-Uthaymin branch of the family gained particular prominence through their religious scholarship, with the family producing notable Islamic scholars who studied under renowned figures like Abd al-Rahman al-Sa'di and Ibn Baz. The name 'Al-Uthaymin' derives from the ancestor Uthman bin Abdullah, who was known by the nickname Uthaymin, meaning 'little Uthman.' Another branch of the broader family produced Muqbil bin Hadi al-Wadi'i, a Yemeni-born Islamic scholar who founded the influential Dar al-Hadith al-Khayriyya seminary.
data.description: A scholarly family from the Banu Tamim tribe, established in Najd's Qaseem region, known for producing prominent Islamic scholars and religious educators.
data.originStory: The Al Muqbil family originates from Ahmad bin Muqbil Al-Tamimi of the Wahabah sub-tribe within the Banu Tamim. The family settled in Unayzah in the Qaseem region of Najd, where they established themselves as a family renowned for religious scholarship.
data.modernStatus: The family maintains its reputation for Islamic scholarship, with descendants continuing to serve as religious educators and having produced internationally recognized Islamic scholars in the 20th and 21st centuries.

folkLegends.title: Ibn Taymiyyah's Lamp Dream
folkLegends.story: Muhammad bin Sulayman, an ancestor of the Al-Uthaymin branch, reportedly had a dream in which he met the renowned Hanbali scholar Ibn Taymiyyah in a market near Unayzah. In this interaction, Ibn Taymiyyah handed him a lamp. Dream interpreters told him this meant he would have a pious child who would achieve an elevated position in religious knowledge. He named his next son Salih, and though Salih was pious but not a scholar, Salih's son Muhammad bin Salih Al-Uthaymin became a renowned Islamic scholar.
folkLegends.source: Family tradition as reported by Abdullah, son of Sheikh Muhammad Al-Uthaymin

data.nameEtymology: Al Muqbil derives from the ancestor Ahmad bin Muqbil. The related surname Al-Uthaymin comes from Uthman bin Abdullah, who was nicknamed 'Uthaymin' meaning 'little Uthman.'

data.history: Based on the research provided, the Al Harbi family mentioned appears to be connected to Khaled bin Ouda bin Mohammed al-Harbi, who was referenced in a November 2001 videotape found by U.S. troops in Afghanistan. In this video, Osama bin Laden discussed what is likely the September 11, 2001 attacks with al-Harbi. The videotape was released by the U.S. on December 13, 2001, and showed bin Laden saying that the number of casualties from the enemy was 'calculated in advance based on the position of the towers.' However, the research does not provide comprehensive information about the Al Harbi family's origins, tribal connections, business activities, or broader historical development. The connection appears to be limited to this individual's association with bin Laden during discussions about the 9/11 attacks, but no further details about the family's background, migration patterns, or establishment in any particular region are provided in the available research.
data.description: Al Harbi family referenced in connection with Khaled bin Ouda bin Mohammed al-Harbi, who appeared in a November 2001 videotape discussing the 9/11 attacks with Osama bin Laden.

data.history: The Al Shathri family traces its documented lineage through Abd al-Aziz ibn Muhammad al-Shathri (1888-1967), a prominent Saudi religious scholar. According to the genealogical record, the family descends from Bani Ziyad, a branch of Amir ibn Sa'sa'a ibn Mu'awiya ibn Bakr ibn Hawazin ibn Mansur ibn Ikrimah ibn Khasfah ibn Qais Aylan ibn Mudar ibn Nizar ibn Ma'ad ibn Adnan. Abd al-Aziz was born in al-Hawta to a merchant family, with his father Muhammad ibn Abd al-Aziz being among the well-known traders of the town. In 1337 AH (1918 CE), King Abd al-Aziz appointed him to serve in the newly established town of al-Rayn for religious instruction, legal judgment, preaching, and teaching. He later moved to Riyadh in 1374 AH (1954 CE) at the invitation of Sheikh Muhammad ibn Ibrahim Al al-Sheikh to teach at the newly founded Imam al-Da'wa Scientific Institute. The family has since established itself prominently in Saudi religious, governmental, and business circles through multiple generations.
data.description: Al Shathri is a Saudi Arabian family of religious scholars and government officials, tracing their lineage to the Hawazin tribal confederation and prominently represented by the 20th-century scholar Abd al-Aziz ibn Muhammad al-Shathri.
data.originStory: The family genealogy traces back through Abd al-Aziz ibn Muhammad al-Shathri to the ancestral line ending with Muhammad ibn Shathir ibn Muhammad ibn Mu'ammal from Bani Ziyad, a branch of the ancient Arabian tribe of Amir ibn Sa'sa'a ibn Mu'awiya ibn Bakr ibn Hawazin.
data.tribalOrigin: Hawazin (through Bani Ziyad branch of Amir ibn Sa'sa'a)
data.modernStatus: The family holds prominent positions in Saudi Arabia's religious and governmental institutions, including members serving as advisors in the Royal Court with ministerial rank, members of the Council of Senior Scholars, military officers, and business leaders.

data.history: The Al Zahrani family originates from the Zahran region in the Al-Baha province of southwestern Saudi Arabia. Based on the available research, the family has deep roots in the Azd tribal confederation, as evidenced by Faris al-Zahrani's full genealogical name which includes 'al-Azdi,' indicating descent from the ancient Azd tribe. The family name 'al-Zahrani' derives from their association with Bilad Zahran (the lands of Zahran) in the mountainous regions of Al-Baha. Members of the family have been documented living in villages such as al-Jawfa and al-Nu'us within the traditional Zahran territories. The research shows family members have pursued various paths in modern times, with some receiving religious education at institutions like the University of King Khaled in Abha, while others have served in government positions, as evidenced by Colonel Talal al-Zahrani who served as a Brigadier General in the Saudi police force.
data.description: Al Zahrani is a Saudi Arabian family originating from the Zahran region in Al-Baha province, with documented Azd tribal ancestry and members distributed across various villages in southwestern Saudi Arabia.
data.originStory: The family traces its origins to Bilad Zahran in the Al-Baha region of southwestern Saudi Arabia, with documented presence in villages including al-Jawfa and al-Nu'us within the traditional Zahran tribal territories.
data.modernStatus: Family members have served in various capacities including government service, with documented presence in Saudi security forces and religious education sectors.
data.nameEtymology: Al-Zahrani derives from Zahran, referring to the Bilad Zahran (lands of Zahran) region in Al-Baha province

data.history: The Al Gurg family traces its origins to Iranian ancestry, specifically from southern Iran, as evidenced by their classification among the Ajam Emiratis - Emiratis of Persian descent. The family's prominence in the UAE began with Easa Saleh Al Gurg, who founded the Easa Saleh Al Gurg Group (ESAG) in 1960 and became recognized as the first Emirati banker and a prominent figure in Emirati diplomacy. He served as Ambassador to the United Kingdom and the Republic of Ireland, establishing the family's dual role in business and diplomacy. The family secured formal recognition under the UAE's 1972 Citizenship Law, which granted Emirati nationality to Iranians residing in the Trucial States prior to 1925 or before the federation's formation in 1971. Over nearly seven decades, ESAG has grown into one of the oldest business family groups in the UAE, operating as a multidivisional conglomerate with over 27 companies across retail, building and construction, industrial sectors, and joint ventures. The family has maintained significant influence in UAE business circles, with current leadership including Raja Easa Al Gurg as chairperson and managing director, Muna Al Gurg as Vice Chairperson, and Easa Al Gurg (grandson of the founder) as Group CEO since 2021.
data.description: Al Gurg is a prominent Emirati business family of Iranian (Ajam) origin, founded by Easa Saleh Al Gurg who established ESAG in 1960 and became the UAE's first Emirati banker. The family operates one of the UAE's oldest business conglomerates with over 27 companies.
data.originStory: The Al Gurg family originates from Iranian ancestry, specifically from southern Iran, making them part of the Ajam Emirati community - Emiratis of Persian descent who trace their ancestral roots to the historical Irahistan region including Fars and Hormozgan provinces.
data.modernStatus: The Al Gurg family currently operates the Easa Saleh Al Gurg Group (ESAG), a multidivisional conglomerate with over 27 companies spanning retail, building and construction, industrial sectors, and joint ventures. They are regional partners for 370 international brands and operate across the UAE, Oman, Asia, Middle East, Africa, parts of America, Australia, and New Zealand. The family holds significant positions including board memberships at Dubai Chamber of Commerce, National Bank of Fujairah, and various educational and charitable institutions.

migrationPath.description: Migration from Iranian ancestry in southern Iran to the UAE, part of the broader Ajam Emirati community movement

data.history: The Al Kathiri (الكثيري) is a major Yemeni tribe based in the southern Arabian Peninsula, constituting one of the largest tribes of Banu Quda'a. They formed natural alliances with Banu Hamdan of Hadramawt in eastern Yemen and Oman, with their influence extending across the region. The Al-Kathiri Sultanate emerged as a significant political entity, extending from Dhofar and Shabwa in the east to the west, reaching north into the Empty Quarter and south to the Arabian Sea coastline, particularly the city of Shahr. Most Hadhrami tribes fell under the protection of the Al-Kathiri Sultanate, though some tribes like Alhmoum, Sheikh Al Amoudi and Yafa rebelled, which the Sultanate successfully overcame. At the beginning of the 19th century, the Qu'aiti Sultanate emerged with British support and began sharing control with the Al-Kathiri Sultanate. The north and east of Hadramout remained under Al-Kathiri control while the west came under Qu'aiti rule. These two sultanates engaged in prolonged wars lasting 100 years until they reconciled and signed a treaty under British colonial supervision in 1937 AD at the Sultan Al-Kathiri's palace in Seiyoun, the Al-Kathiri capital.
data.description: The Al Kathiri is one of the largest tribes of Banu Quda'a based in southern Arabia, particularly Yemen and Oman, who established a major sultanate in Hadramawt that lasted until the 20th century. They have extensive populations across the Gulf states, Southeast Asia, and East Africa.
data.originStory: Genetic studies of haplogroup J1 L222.2 suggest that tribes from Quda'ah, including Al Kathiri, are more closely aligned with the Adnanites, challenging earlier claims of Qahtanite origins that arose during the early Islamic period.
data.modernStatus: The Al Kathiri maintain presence across Yemen, Oman, UAE, Saudi Arabia, Qatar, with populations in Southeast Asia and East Africa including Somalia, Kenya, Tanzania, and Indonesia.

data.history: Based on the research provided, Al Falahi appears to be referenced only in the context of the House of Nahyan's genealogy. According to Wikipedia sources on the House of Nahyan, the ruling family of Abu Dhabi is described as 'a branch of the House of Al Falahi (Āl Bū Falāḥ), a branch of the Bani Yas tribe.' The research indicates that the Bani Yas came to Abu Dhabi in the 18th century from Liwa Oasis, and that the Al Nahyan family has ruled Abu Dhabi since 1793, previously ruling Liwa. The House of Al Falahi is also noted to be related to the House of Al Falasi, from which the ruling family of Dubai (the Al Maktoum) descends. However, the research provides no specific independent information about the Al Falahi family beyond their connection to the Al Nahyan lineage. The only other reference to the Al Falahi name appears in connection with Mohammed Ateeq Al-Falahi, who serves as secretary general of the Red Crescent Society of the United Arab Emirates and chairman of the Zayed House for Islamic Culture, though no family connection is explicitly established in the research.
data.description: Al Falahi is mentioned in research as the parent house from which the ruling Al Nahyan family of Abu Dhabi descends, described as a branch of the Bani Yas tribe.
data.originStory: The research indicates that Al Falahi is part of the Bani Yas tribal structure, with the Bani Yas having come to Abu Dhabi in the 18th century from Liwa Oasis, but provides no specific origin story for the Al Falahi branch itself.
data.modernStatus: The research provides no specific information about the current status of the Al Falahi family as a distinct entity, only their historical connection to the Al Nahyan ruling family.

data.history: The Al Ajmi family represents a prominent Kuwaiti lineage with documented origins tracing to the Arabian Ajman tribe, specifically the Al Sulaiman branch. According to Wikipedia sources, Sheikh Ahmad bin Ali Al-Ajmi's surname indicates his belonging to this tribal branch, clarifying that despite the name 'Ajmi' (which can mean 'non-Arab'), the family is of Arab origin from the Ajman tribe. The family has established significant wealth through Kuwait's oil and construction industries, as evidenced by Hajjaj bin Fahd al-Ajmi being described as descending from a wealthy Kuwaiti family with earnings from these sectors. The family has expanded beyond Kuwait through marriage alliances, notably with the Bahraini royal family - King Hamad bin Isa Al Khalifa's second wife, Sheikha Sheia bint Hassan Al Khrayyesh Al Ajmi, is from Kuwait and has borne him two sons including Sheikh Nasser bin Hamad Al Khalifa. This connection has elevated the family's regional prominence and established important political ties across the Gulf states.
data.description: A prominent Kuwaiti family of Arabian Ajman tribal origin, known for wealth from oil and construction industries and significant marriage alliances with Gulf royal families, particularly Bahrain's ruling Al Khalifa dynasty.
data.originStory: The Al Ajmi family originates from the Arabian Ajman tribe, specifically the Al Sulaiman branch. Despite their surname suggesting non-Arab origins, scholarly sources confirm their Arab ancestry from this well-established Arabian tribal confederation.
data.modernStatus: The family maintains significant wealth through oil and construction industries in Kuwait, with members holding various professional roles including religious scholarship, business, and sports. Their marriage alliance with Bahrain's ruling family has elevated their regional political influence.
data.nameEtymology: The surname 'Al-Ajmi' traditionally refers to non-Arab origins (Persian/foreign), but in this family's case, it specifically indicates descent from the Arabian Ajman tribe's Al Sulaiman branch, representing a tribal affiliation rather than ethnic origin.

data.history: Al Mahooz appears to be primarily associated with a district in Bahrain rather than a distinct family lineage. The name is most prominently connected to Mahooz, a neighborhood in Bahrain where several significant historical and religious sites are located. The 13th-century Bahraini Twelver Shia theologian Kamal al-Din Maytham ibn Ali al-Bahrani (1238–1299) is buried in Mahooz, where a shrine and mosque have been constructed in his honor. Al-Bahrani was a leading scholar who was part of a thirteenth-century Bahrain school of theology that emphasized rationalism, and his scholarship influenced both Imami and Sunni sources. The area of Mahooz has historically served as a burial ground, with notable figures like Abdulaziz Jassim Kanoo (1931-2016) also being interred in Manama Cemetery. The district appears in various historical contexts as a residential area that has been incorporated into greater Manama's urban expansion, with roads connecting it to other villages and serving as endpoints for transportation routes during Bahrain's development in the 20th century.
data.description: Al Mahooz refers to a district in Bahrain known primarily for religious and burial sites, including the shrine of 13th-century theologian Maytham al-Bahrani. The area has historical significance as part of Manama's urban development.
data.modernStatus: Mahooz remains a district within the Capital Governorate of Bahrain, serving as a residential area and location of important religious sites including shrines and cemeteries.

data.history:
The Al Hajri family is part of the Bani Hajer (Al-Hajri) tribe, which belongs to the larger Qahtanite tribal confederation. Bani Hajer originated from the Abida region near Sarat Abida (also known as 'alhirja') near Thahran Al Janoub in Yemen. Led by Sheikh Mohammed Bin Shaba'an in the early 13th century, they migrated to Lower Najd. Under Sheikh Shafi bin Safar bin Mohammed Bin Shaba'an, they made a second migration towards the eastern village of Al Ahsa'a in Saudi Arabia. Some members of the tribe ventured into Qatar and other Gulf states, where they have resided since.

The Al Hajri family gained significant prominence through their connection to the Qatari royal family. Sheikh Mana bin Abdul Hadi Al Hajri served as Qatar's Ambassador to Jordan from 2003 through 2011. His daughter Al-Anoud bint Mana Al Hajri married Sheikh Tamim bin Hamad Al Thani (now Emir of Qatar) on 3 March 2009, becoming his second wife. This marriage elevated the family's status within Qatari society and connected them directly to the ruling Al Thani family.

The family has demonstrated significant business acumen and wealth. Abdulhadi Mana Al-Hajri, son of Sheikh Mana, has become a prominent businessman and investor. In March 2020, he purchased the Ritz Hotel in London for an estimated £700 million, making it the most expensive hotel sale in the world. He has also made substantial property investments, including purchasing a £37.5 million townhouse in London's Mayfair district in 2023 and the Erbilginler Yalı mansion in Istanbul for over $100 million in 2015.
data.description: The Al Hajri family is a prominent Qatari family of Bani Hajer tribal origin, known for their diplomatic service and direct marriage connection to the ruling Al Thani family. They have achieved significant wealth through business investments, particularly in luxury real estate.
data.originStory: The Al Hajri family traces its origins to the Bani Hajer tribe, which originated from the Abida region near Sarat Abida in Yemen. The tribe migrated to Lower Najd in the early 13th century under Sheikh Mohammed Bin Shaba'an, then later moved to Al Ahsa'a in eastern Saudi Arabia under Sheikh Shafi bin Safar. Some members eventually settled in Qatar and other Gulf states.
data.modernStatus: Highly prominent in Qatar through marriage to the Emir and significant business investments. Abdulhadi Mana Al-Hajri owns the Ritz Hotel London and has extensive international property holdings. Al-Anoud bint Mana Al Hajri is the second wife of Emir Tamim bin Hamad Al Thani and mother of five of his children.
data.nameEtymology: Al Hajri derives from Bani Hajer, also known as al-Hawajir, meaning 'Family surnames: Al-Hajri' according to the tribal naming convention.

data.history: The Al Jabri family name appears across different regions of the Arab world with notable figures in Saudi Arabia, Syria, and Morocco, though direct genealogical connections between these branches are not established in the available research. In Saudi Arabia, Saad bin Khalid Al Jabri emerged as a prominent security official, spending his early years in Ha'il before enrolling at a police academy following his father's death. He rose through the ranks to become a major-general, minister of state, and long-time adviser to Crown Prince Muhammad bin Nayef, playing a crucial role in Saudi Arabia's counter-terrorism efforts and serving as a key liaison with Western intelligence agencies including the Five Eyes alliance. His career peaked when he helped transform and modernize Saudi security services, with reforms credited as instrumental in foiling major terrorist plots including the 2010 transatlantic aircraft bomb plot. However, his position became precarious when Mohammed bin Salman perceived him as plotting with bin Nayef against him, leading to his dismissal from government positions in September 2015 and eventual exile to Canada in May 2017. Since then, he has faced Saudi government efforts at extradition, alleged assassination attempts, and the detention of his children as leverage to force his return.
data.description: The Al Jabri name appears among notable families across the Arab world, with the most documented branch being Saudi security officials who played key roles in counter-terrorism before facing political persecution under Mohammed bin Salman's rise to power.
data.modernStatus: The Saudi branch faces severe persecution with Saad Al Jabri in exile in Canada since 2017, his children detained in Saudi Arabia as hostages, and the family subject to extradition attempts and alleged assassination plots by the Mohammed bin Salman regime.

data.history: The Al Zadjali family name derives from the Jadgali people, an Indo-Aryan ethnic group originally from the western Indian subcontinent who subsequently migrated to various regions including Balochistan and Oman. The Jadgali people, also known as Nummaṛ, speak the Jadgali language (also called Zadjali or Zidgali), which is a dialect of Sindhi most closely related to Lasi. When Arabs arrived in modern-day Sindh and Baluchistan, they encountered the Jadgal at the coast of Makran, where the Arabic name 'az-Zighālī' or 'az-Zijālī' originated. According to historical accounts, some Jadgal communities left their homeland after a defeat from the ruler of Sindh and moved westward, eventually settling in various regions including Dashtyari during the reign of Shah Abbas. In Oman, immigrant Jadgal communities became established and are known by the Arabic designation az-zighālī or az-zijālī. The Al Zadjali family in Oman has produced notable contemporary figures including artist Maryam Al Zadjali, who serves as Director of the Omani Society for Fine Arts, and Baida Al Zadjali, who became the first Omani woman in modern times to walk the Oman Empty Quarter as part of the 'Her Faces of Change' expedition in 2018.
data.description: The Al Zadjali family traces its origins to the Jadgali people, an Indo-Aryan ethnic group from the Indian subcontinent who migrated to various regions including Oman. The family has produced notable contemporary figures in arts and exploration.
data.originStory: The Al Zadjali name derives from the Jadgali people, an Indo-Aryan ethnic group originally from the western Indian subcontinent. The Jadgal communities migrated westward after various historical events, with some settling in Oman where they became known as az-zighālī or az-zijālī.
data.modernStatus: The family has established itself in Oman with members active in arts and cultural leadership. Maryam Al Zadjali serves as Director of the Omani Society for Fine Arts since 2008, while other family members have participated in notable expeditions and cultural projects.
data.nameEtymology: Al Zadjali derives from 'Zadjali', one of the alternative names for the Jadgali language and people, which in turn comes from the Arabic designation 'az-Zijālī' used to refer to the Jadgal communities in Oman and the UAE.

migrationPath.description: Migration of Jadgal communities from the Indian subcontinent to various regions including Oman, where they became known as az-zighālī

data.history: Based on the available research, the Banaja family appears to have significant business connections in Saudi Arabia, particularly through Banaja KSA Holding Company. According to Reuters reporting from December 2017, Banaja KSA Holding Company was a major partner with GlaxoSmithKline (GSK) in their Saudi Arabian pharmaceutical operations. The partnership was substantial enough that GSK had to increase its stake from 49% to 75% to take control from Banaja KSA Holding Company, indicating the Banaja family held a controlling interest in this significant pharmaceutical venture. Additionally, historical records from Al-Balad, Jeddah's historic district, mention 'Dar Al Banaja' as one of the notable historical houses in the Al Sham neighborhood, suggesting the family has had a long-standing presence in the Hejaz region of Saudi Arabia. The mention of their house alongside other prominent merchant families' residences in Jeddah's historic commercial center indicates they were likely involved in the traditional trading networks that made Jeddah a major commercial hub.
data.description: The Banaja family appears to be a prominent Saudi business family with historical roots in Jeddah and modern pharmaceutical industry partnerships.
data.modernStatus: The family operates through Banaja KSA Holding Company, which was a major partner with GlaxoSmithKline in Saudi pharmaceutical operations until 2017, when GSK increased its stake to gain controlling interest.

timelineEvents.description: GSK increased its stake in Saudi operations from 49% to 75%, taking control from Banaja KSA Holding Company

connections.context: Banaja KSA Holding Company was GSK's major partner in Saudi pharmaceutical operations until 2017

data.history: The Al Kabeer family is a cadet branch of the Saudi royal family, descended from Saud Al Kabeer. The lineage traces through Saud Al Kabeer to his son Mohammed bin Saud Al Kabeer, and then to Sultan bin Mohammed, who served as chairman of Almarai Company until March 2020. The family has been involved in significant business ventures, particularly in the food and agricultural sector through Almarai, which Sultan bin Mohammed founded in 1977 and transformed into a public company in 2005. The Al Kabeer branch has faced political challenges, including the detention of several family members in January 2018 due to protests over the cancellation of state payments for utilities and the execution of their cousin Prince Turki bin Saud Al Kabeer in 2016. The family has maintained extensive business interests across telecommunications, cement, petrochemicals, and insurance sectors.
data.description: A cadet branch of the Saudi royal family descended from Saud Al Kabeer, known for founding and leading major Saudi businesses, particularly Almarai Company.
data.originStory: The Al Kabeer family traces its origins to Saud Al Kabeer as the founding patriarch, with the lineage continuing through his son Mohammed bin Saud Al Kabeer and grandson Sultan bin Mohammed.
data.modernStatus: Major business family with extensive holdings in food production (Almarai), telecommunications (Zain Saudi), cement (Al Yamamah), and other sectors. Current generation led by Nayef bin Sultan Al Kabeer as Almarai chairman since March 2020.
data.nameEtymology: Al Kabeer means 'the Great' or 'the Elder' in Arabic

data.history:
The Al Uthaymeen family traces its lineage to the well-known Banu Tamim tribe, specifically descended from the Wahabah sub-tribe. The family's surname Al-Uthaymin derives from their great-great-great-grandfather Uthman bin Abdullah, who was known by the nickname Uthaymin, meaning 'little Uthman.' The family's documented genealogy traces back through Muhammad bin Salih bin Muhammad bin Sulayman bin Abdurrahman bin Uthman bin Abdullah bin Abdurrahmaan bin Ahmad bin Muqbil, with their ancestor Ahmad bin Muqbil Al-Tamimi being from the Wahabah sub-tribe of Banu Tamim.

The Al-Uthaymin family became established in the town of Unayzah in the Qaseem Region of the Najd Province, where they gained recognition for their scholarship in religious affairs. The family's prominence in Islamic scholarship became particularly notable through Sheikh Muhammad ibn Salih al-Uthaymin (1929-2001), who emerged as one of Saudi Arabia's most influential Islamic scholars. According to family tradition, Muhammad bin Sulayman (the great-grandfather) had a prophetic dream where he met the renowned Hanbali scholar Ibn Taymiyyah, who handed him a lamp, which scholars interpreted as a sign that his lineage would produce a great Islamic scholar.

The family's modern prominence is primarily associated with religious scholarship rather than business or political power. Sheikh Muhammad ibn Salih al-Uthaymin studied under prominent scholars including Abd al-Rahman al-Sa'di and Ibn Baz, eventually becoming a major religious authority who authored numerous Islamic texts and taught students from around the world until his death in 2001 at King Faisal Specialist Hospital in Jeddah.
data.description: The Al Uthaymeen family is a scholarly family from the Banu Tamim tribe, based in Unayzah, Qaseem Region, most famous for producing the prominent 20th-century Islamic scholar Sheikh Muhammad ibn Salih al-Uthaymin. They are known primarily for their contributions to Islamic scholarship and religious education.
data.originStory: The family originates from the Wahabah sub-tribe of the Banu Tamim, with their ancestor Ahmad bin Muqbil Al-Tamimi establishing their tribal lineage. The family name derives from Uthman bin Abdullah, nicknamed 'Uthaymin' (little Uthman), who was the great-great-great-grandfather of the most famous family member.
data.modernStatus: The family is primarily known for religious scholarship rather than business or political influence. Their most prominent member, Sheikh Muhammad ibn Salih al-Uthaymin, was one of Saudi Arabia's leading Islamic scholars until his death in 2001, with his teachings and writings continuing to influence Islamic education globally.

folkLegends.title: Ibn Taymiyyah's Lamp Dream
folkLegends.story: Muhammad bin Sulayman, the great-grandfather of Sheikh Muhammad, had a dream in which he met the renowned Hanbali scholar Ibn Taymiyyah in a market near Unayzah. Ibn Taymiyyah handed him a lamp, which dream interpreters said meant he would have a descendant who would be pious and elevated in religious knowledge. This prophecy was believed fulfilled through his great-grandson Sheikh Muhammad ibn Salih al-Uthaymin.
folkLegends.source: Family oral tradition as reported by Sheikh Muhammad's son Abdullah

data.nameEtymology: Al-Uthaymin derives from Uthman bin Abdullah, who was known by the nickname 'Uthaymin,' meaning 'little Uthman.' The family adopted this as their surname from this ancestor who was the great-great-great-grandfather of Sheikh Muhammad ibn Salih al-Uthaymin.

data.history: The Al Ghamdi family originates from the Ghamid tribe, which is based in the Al Bahah Province of southwestern Saudi Arabia. This region, nestled between Mecca and Abha, is noted as one of the Kingdom's prime tourist attractions and has historically been home to the Ghamid tribal confederation. The family shares tribal affiliation across the broader Ghamid community, which includes various branches throughout the southwestern Arabian Peninsula. Members of the Al Ghamdi family have been documented in various roles throughout modern Saudi history, from religious scholarship to other occupations. The family name appears in historical records spanning multiple generations, with documented presence in the Al Bahah region extending back several decades. Contemporary members of the family have been involved in various sectors of Saudi society, maintaining connections to their ancestral homeland in the southwestern provinces.

data.history: The Al Malki family name appears across multiple Arab regions with distinct origins. In Syria, the family traces to North African Ulama trained in the Maliki school of jurisprudence, exemplified by Adnan al-Malki (1918-1955), a prominent Syrian military officer who served as deputy-chief of staff and played a key role in Ba'ath Party formation. His family was described as wealthy and prestigious Damascenes. In the Gulf region, several contemporary Al Malki figures have emerged in different fields - Amal Al-Malki became a pioneering Qatari academic, while Fayez Al-Malki gained prominence as a Saudi television actor and humanitarian. The name also appears among Saudi religious reformist circles with Hassan al-Maliki, though his specific family connections remain unclear from available sources.
data.description: Al Malki appears to be multiple distinct family lines across the Arab world, with verified Syrian origins from North African Maliki scholars and contemporary presence in Gulf states in academic, entertainment, and religious spheres.
data.originStory: The Syrian Al Malki line originated from North African Ulama who were trained in the Maliki school of Islamic jurisprudence, settling in Damascus where they became a wealthy and prestigious family.
data.modernStatus: Contemporary Al Malki family members hold positions in academia (Qatar University system), entertainment industry (Saudi television), and religious scholarship, with no single unified business or political entity.
data.nameEtymology: Likely derived from 'Maliki' referring to the Maliki school of Islamic jurisprudence, one of the four major Sunni legal schools.

data.history:
The Ba Wazeer family traces its origins to the Hadhramaut region of Yemen, where they are documented as part of the Mashaikh tribes alongside other prominent families like the Al-Amoudi and Ba'Abbad. According to historical sources, Sheikh Sa'eed ibn Isa Al-Amoudi married the daughter of Ahmed ibn Sa'eed Ba' Wa'ar of the Banu Afif of the Kindah tribe in the 13th century, establishing early connections between these influential Hadhrami families. The Ba Wazeer are recognized as one of the most important families among the Mashaikh tribes of Hadhramaut, holding significant religious and social influence in the region second only to the Ba'Alawi sada in terms of prestige.

The family's prominence extends beyond their traditional Hadhrami homeland, with members playing significant roles in various political movements across the Arab world. Most notably, the family gained international recognition through Palestinian political figure Intissar al-Wazir, born in Gaza City in 1941, who became a pioneering female member of the Fatah organization in 1959 and served as Minister of Social Affairs of the Palestinian National Authority from 1994 to 2005. Her husband was Khalil al-Wazir (Abu Jihad), a senior figure in the Palestine Liberation Organization who was assassinated by Israel in 1988.

The Ba Wazeer family's influence spans both religious scholarship and modern political activism, representing the broader diaspora of Hadhrami families who have established themselves in various parts of the Middle East and beyond. Their historical roots in the tribal confederations of Hadhramaut, combined with their adaptation to modern political and social movements, exemplifies the evolution of traditional Gulf and Yemeni families in the contemporary era.
data.description: The Ba Wazeer family is a prominent Hadhrami family from Yemen, recognized as one of the most important among the Mashaikh tribes of Hadhramaut. They have achieved international prominence through political figures like Palestinian leader Intissar al-Wazir.
data.originStory: The Ba Wazeer family originates from the Hadhramaut region of Yemen, where they are part of the traditional Mashaikh tribal confederation. Historical records show their integration into prominent Hadhrami society through marriages with other influential families, including connections to the Banu Afif of the Kindah tribe in the 13th century.
data.modernStatus: The family maintains significant influence in Hadhrami society and has achieved international recognition through political leadership, particularly in Palestinian politics. Members have held high-level government positions including ministerial roles in the Palestinian National Authority.

data.history:
The Al Kabli family name appears to have origins connected to the Hejaz region and Afghanistan, based on available evidence from notable figures. The most prominent documented member is Abdel Karim al Kabli (1932-2021), a renowned Sudanese singer-songwriter born in Port Sudan who became one of Sudan's most celebrated cultural figures. His name 'al Kabli' suggests potential connections to Kabul or the broader Khorasan region, though the exact family origins are not definitively established in available records.

The family name also appears in connection with Islamic scholarship and cultural activities across different regions. In Sudan, Abdel Karim al Kabli's legacy established the family's prominence in arts and humanitarian work, particularly through his advocacy for peace in Darfur and South Sudan, and his role as a UN Goodwill Ambassador. His work bridged traditional Islamic cultural heritage with modern artistic expression, suggesting the family maintained strong connections to both religious scholarship and cultural innovation.

The geographical spread of the Al Kabli name, appearing in contexts from Afghanistan to Sudan to the Arabian Peninsula, indicates possible historical migration patterns typical of scholarly or merchant families in the Islamic world, though specific migration details are not documented in available sources.
data.description: A family name with apparent connections to the Hejaz region and Afghanistan, most notably represented by Sudanese cultural icon Abdel Karim al Kabli (1932-2021), suggesting possible scholarly or cultural heritage across the Islamic world.
data.modernStatus: The family is most prominently known through Abdel Karim al Kabli's cultural and humanitarian legacy in Sudan, including his work as a UN Goodwill Ambassador and his influence on Sudanese music and peace advocacy.
data.nameEtymology: The name 'al Kabli' likely derives from 'Kabul' or the broader Khorasan region, as suggested by the alias 'Julaybib al-Kabli' used by an Afghan operative, indicating geographical origins in Afghanistan or surrounding areas.

data.history: Al Balochi refers to families and individuals of Baloch ethnic origin who have settled in the Gulf states. The Baloch people are an ethnic group primarily inhabiting the Balochistan region spanning modern-day Pakistan, Iran, and Afghanistan, speaking Balochi, a Northwestern Iranian language. According to research, there are speakers of Balochi in Oman and the rest of the Arab states of the Persian Gulf, indicating historical migration and settlement patterns. The case of Ammar al-Baluchi illustrates one such family trajectory - born in Al Ahmadi, Kuwait in 1977 to a family that was ethnically Baluch, originally from Balochistan, Pakistan. His extended family moved to Kuwait in the 1960s, with the family patriarch and his brother being lay Deobandi preachers who relocated the family from their original homeland. Historical records indicate that Balochi slaves were trafficked to Saudi Arabia via Oman and the Gulf states in the 1930s and 1940s, with some reports suggesting that some Baluchis sold themselves or their children to slave traders to escape poverty, contributing to the Baloch presence in the region through tragic circumstances.
data.description: Al Balochi represents families of Baloch ethnic origin who migrated to the Gulf states, primarily from the Balochistan region of Pakistan, Iran, and Afghanistan, with documented presence since at least the 1960s.
data.originStory: The Al Balochi families originate from the Balochistan region spanning modern-day Pakistan, Iran, and Afghanistan. The family patriarch and his brother were lay Deobandi preachers who moved their family to Kuwait in the 1960s, representing voluntary migration for economic and religious opportunities.
data.modernStatus: The research indicates ongoing presence of Baloch communities in Gulf states, with some families having established themselves over multiple generations since the 1960s migration wave.

data.history: Al Zulfi (also spelled Az Zulfi, Zulfi, or Az Zilfi) is primarily a city and governorate in Riyadh Province, Saudi Arabia, located approximately 260 kilometers northwest of Riyadh in the heart of the historical Najd region. The city has ancient roots, with some sources identifying it with a settlement mentioned by historical chroniclers as dating back to the third century AH. During the Pre-Islamic era, the settlement was called 'zulifit' or 'alkrm' and was reportedly founded by the tribes of Banu Hanifa and Banu Tamim. The area fell under various rulers throughout history, including the Ukhaydhirites who moved their capital from Hajr to nearby Al Kharj, and later became part of the expanding Saudi state. In the late 19th century (around 1090 AH/late 19th century CE), Al-Zulfi was established in its modern form by the Al-Asaadh clan of Talha with the Al-Rokh branch of the Otaiba tribe, known as Al-Rashid and Al-Farahid, descendants of Rashid Mohammed and Saleh Rashid sons of Farhud Al-Asaadi. The city played a role in various historical conflicts, including battles during the Saudi state expansion and was involved in the broader political developments of the Najd region. A significant connection exists between Al-Zulfi and Kuwait, as many prominent Kuwaiti families, including the ruling Al-Sabah family and the prominent Al-Shaya and Al-Ghanim families, hail from Al-Zulfi, having emigrated in the 19th and early 20th centuries.
data.description: Al Zulfi is a city and governorate in Riyadh Province, Saudi Arabia, with historical roots dating to the third century AH, notable for being the ancestral home of several prominent Kuwaiti families including the ruling Al-Sabah dynasty.
data.originStory: According to historical sources, the modern city of Al-Zulfi was established in the late 19th century (approximately 1090 AH) by the Al-Asaadh clan of Talha working with the Al-Rokh branch of the Otaiba tribe, specifically descendants known as Al-Rashid and Al-Farahid, who were sons of Rashid Mohammed and Saleh Rashid, themselves sons of Farhud Al-Asaadi.
data.modernStatus: Al-Zulfi currently serves as a governorate in Riyadh Province, Saudi Arabia, with a population of 68,317 as of 2022. The city is a commercial and industrial hub with over 7,000 commercial licenses and more than 35 industrial facilities producing various goods for both domestic and international markets, exporting to over 25 countries worldwide.
data.nameEtymology: The etymology of the name Al-Zulfi is unclear with multiple proposed explanations. One hypothesis suggests it derives from Al-Zlifat, related to the Tuwaiq Mountains. Another theory proposes it refers to the villages surrounding Al-Zulfi as documented by Ibn Balihad. Some suggest it originated from 'Izdelaf,' an Arabic word denoting movement from one location to another.

data.history: The Al Sudairi family are part of the Dawasir tribe settled in Al Ghat, an oasis town located in central Arabia nearly 250 kilometers northwest of Riyadh. The family rose to prominence in the 19th century when Ahmed bin Muhammad bin Turki bin Suleiman Al Sudairi (nicknamed Ahmed Al Kabeer) was assigned by Faisal bin Turki, ruler of the Second Saudi State, to serve as administrator in different regions including Al Ahsa, Al Ghat, and Buraimi before his death in 1860. His daughter Sara bint Ahmed Al Sudairi became the mother of King Abdulaziz (Ibn Saud), the founder of Saudi Arabia, through her marriage to Abdul Rahman bin Faisal. In the 20th century, another branch of the family gained exceptional prominence when Ahmed bin Mohammed Al Sudairi (1869-1936) became a powerful chief of the Sudairi tribe and early supporter of King Abdulaziz during his conquest of Saudi Arabia. His daughter Hussa bint Ahmed Al Sudairi married King Abdulaziz and became the mother of seven sons known as the 'Sudairi Seven,' including future kings Fahd and Salman, establishing the Al Sudairi as one of the most influential families in the Saudi royal structure.
data.description: The Al Sudairi are a prominent family within the Dawasir tribe, originally settled in Al Ghat oasis in central Arabia. They became one of the most influential families in Saudi Arabia through intermarriage with the Al Saud royal family, producing multiple kings and crown princes.
data.originStory: The Al Sudairi family are part of the noble Dawasir tribe and were settled in Al Ghat, an oasis town in central Arabia approximately 250 kilometers northwest of Riyadh.
data.modernStatus: The Al Sudairi remain one of the most powerful families in Saudi Arabia through the royal lineage known as the 'Sudairi Seven.' Multiple family members have served as provincial governors, with King Salman currently ruling as the seventh monarch of Saudi Arabia since 2015.

data.history: Al Jazeera is not a traditional GCC family but rather a media company founded in 1996 as part of Qatar's efforts to transform economic power into political influence in the Arab world and beyond. The network was established with political and financial backing from the government of Qatar, with its headquarters in Doha. Al Jazeera began as an Arabic news channel and later expanded to include Al Jazeera English and other services. The network has faced numerous controversies and restrictions across various countries since its inception, with governments accusing it of bias and serving Qatari political interests. In 2013, Al Jazeera expanded into the United States market by purchasing Current TV and launching Al Jazeera America, though this venture was short-lived and closed in April 2016 due to low viewership and financial pressures. The organization continues to receive funding from Qatar and has been criticized as effectively functioning as Qatari state media, with various staff resignations citing editorial interference aligned with Qatar's foreign policy objectives.
data.description: Al Jazeera Media Network is a Qatar-based media company founded in 1996, operating news channels and digital platforms with political and financial backing from the Qatari government.
data.originStory: Al Jazeera was founded in 1996 as part of Qatari efforts to turn economic power into political influence in the Arab world and beyond, continuing to receive political and financial backing from the government of Qatar.
data.modernStatus: Major international media network headquartered in Doha, Qatar, operating Al Jazeera Arabic, Al Jazeera English, and various digital platforms, though facing restrictions and bans in multiple countries due to alleged bias and political controversies.

data.history: The Bin Drai family established the first polo club in Dubai in 1974, making them pioneers in bringing the sport of polo to the emirate. This early involvement in equestrian sports positioned the family as influential figures in Dubai's sporting and social circles during the early years of the UAE's formation. The family's polo legacy continues today through Saeed Bin Drai, who serves as patron of the Bin Drai Polo Team, which is based at the Desert Palm polo club in Dubai. The team competes in various tournaments including the Cartier International Dubai Polo Challenge, the UAE Nations Cup, and the Sunset Polo International Cup, maintaining the family's longstanding connection to the sport that helped establish Dubai's reputation in international polo circles.

data.history: The Al Romaithi family's modern prominence is documented through Ahmed Ali Matar Al Romaithi's position as a board member of Etihad Airways, the UAE's flag carrier airline established in 2003. His appointment to the board of directors of this major state-owned enterprise indicates the family's integration into the UAE's contemporary business and governmental structure. Etihad Airways was founded by Sheikh Ahmed bin Saif Al Nahyan with AED 500 million in startup capital and has grown to become the second-largest airline in the UAE after Emirates. The airline's governance structure includes seven independent non-executive board members, with Al Romaithi serving alongside other prominent Emirati business figures. This corporate leadership role suggests the Al Romaithi family has established significant standing within Abu Dhabi's modern economic framework, particularly in the aviation and state enterprise sectors.

data.history: The Al Ghanem family has a complex history spanning multiple countries in the Gulf region and Levant. The family name 'Ghanem' is descended from Jafna ibn Amr of the Azd Dynasty, one of the oldest Pre-Islamic communities that inhabited southwestern Arabia, mainly Yemen, and the Al-Bahah and 'Asir provinces in Saudi Arabia. Following the Ma'rib dam break during the 3rd century, they scattered between modern-day Saudi Arabia and the Levant. Jafna settled in Roman-ruled Syria and established the Kingdom of the Ghassanids, which disappeared in 635 when the area was conquered by Muslims. In Lebanon, the family descends directly from Moussa Ghanem Al-Ghassani, a native of Al-Nabek, Syria and a descendant of the Ghassanid Dynasty, who moved to Yanouh in the 9th century and settled there. The Al-Ghanem family is historically dominant in Kuwait and Bahrain, being part of the Bani Utub federation which originally founded Kuwait. The Bani Utub elite includes the Al Sabah ruling family. In Kuwait, the family has produced prominent political and business leaders, including Ali Mohammed Thunayan Al-Ghanim, chairman of Ali Alghanim & Sons Company, and his brother Fouad Mohammed Thunayan Alghanim, chairman of Fouad Alghanim & Sons Group of Companies. The family has also produced notable political figures like Marzouq Al-Ghanim, who served as speaker of the Kuwaiti National Assembly.
data.description: The Al Ghanem family is a prominent Gulf merchant family with historical roots tracing back to the Ghassanid Kingdom of Syria and significant presence in Kuwait, Bahrain, and other Gulf states, particularly known for their business enterprises and political leadership in Kuwait.
data.originStory: The Ghanem family is descended from Jafna ibn Amr of the Azd Dynasty, one of the oldest Pre-Islamic communities that inhabited southwestern Arabia. Following the Ma'rib dam break during the 3rd century, they scattered between modern-day Saudi Arabia and the Levant. Jafna settled in Roman-ruled Syria and established the Kingdom of the Ghassanids, which disappeared in 635 when conquered by Muslims.
data.modernStatus: The Al-Ghanim family operates major business conglomerates in Kuwait including Ali Alghanim & Sons Company (BMW and Land Rover dealership) and Fouad Alghanim & Sons Group of Companies with subsidiaries in the U.S., Europe, Africa and the Middle East. Family members hold prominent political positions, with Marzouq Al-Ghanim having served as Speaker of the Kuwaiti National Assembly from 2013.
data.nameEtymology: The name 'Ghanem' is derived from the Arabic root meaning 'successful' or 'wealthy'. The family was named after a spring of water (Ghassan) where they stopped on their way to Syria, hence becoming known as Ghassanids.

data.history: The Al Qallaf family appears to have Baharna origins, part of the Shia Muslim community indigenous to the historical region of Bahrain. According to research on Baharna migrations, prominent families including al-Qallāf moved to Kuwait during the 18th and 19th centuries as part of successive waves of migration from Bahrain to Kuwait, where their skills as craftsmen and merchants became highly valued during Kuwait's commercial expansion. The family name appears among traditional Bahrani families known for crafts and trades. The most prominent modern member is Hussain Ali Alsayyid Khalifa Hussain al-Qallaf, born in 1958, who studied Sharia law and was elected to the Kuwaiti National Assembly in 1996 representing the first district. He affiliates with the Shia deputies bloc and has been involved in various political debates including criticism of government policies on education, banking bailouts, and parliamentary procedures. Historical records indicate that Danish explorer Carsten Niebuhr recorded the presence of Bahrani families in Kuwait as early as 1765, noting their involvement in the pearl-diving trade, suggesting the Al Qallaf family's presence in Kuwait may date back to this early period.
data.description: A Kuwaiti family of likely Baharna Shia origins, known for political representation through Hussain al-Qallaf, former member of the National Assembly who served from 1996.
data.originStory: The Al Qallaf family appears to be part of the Baharna community that migrated from Bahrain to Kuwait during the 18th-19th century waves of migration, joining other traditional Bahrani families known for crafts and trades.

data.history: The Al Hasawi family is a prominent Kuwaiti merchant family that has played significant roles in business and sports ownership in the Gulf region. Fawaz Mubarak Al-Hasawi, born on October 25, 1968, represents the modern generation of this influential family. Between 2010 and 2012, Fawaz served as president of Qadsia SC, one of Kuwait's largest sporting clubs, where his tenure was highly successful with the club winning two Kuwait Premier League titles, two Emir Cups, the Federation Cup, and the Super Cup. In July 2012, the Al-Hasawi family made international headlines by purchasing Nottingham Forest Football Club from the estate of the late Nigel Doughty, with Fawaz initially serving alongside his cousin Omar Al-Hasawi before taking over as chairman in December 2012. The family's ownership of Nottingham Forest lasted until May 2017, when they sold the club to Greek shipping magnate Evangelos Marinakis, marking the end of a tumultuous five-year period that saw the club decline in league position every season under their ownership.
data.description: The Al Hasawi family is a Kuwaiti merchant family known for their involvement in sports ownership and business ventures, most notably through Fawaz Al-Hasawi's ownership of Nottingham Forest Football Club from 2012 to 2017.
data.modernStatus: The family maintains business interests in Kuwait and was involved in international football club ownership. Fawaz Al-Hasawi previously owned Nottingham Forest FC and served as president of Qadsia SC in Kuwait.

data.history:
The Al Shayji family appears to be a Kuwaiti family with documented presence in Kuwait's political and cultural spheres. The most prominent documented member is Abdulaziz Al-Shayji, born in 1967, who studied architectural engineering at the University of Miami and became a member of the Kuwaiti National Assembly representing the third district. He was elected to the National Assembly in 2008 and affiliated with the Islamist Hadas party, though political parties are technically illegal in Kuwait.

The family has also been involved in Kuwait's cultural and literary scene. Adnan Al-Shayji served as the first secretary general of the Foundation of Abdulaziz Saud Al-Babtain's Prize for Poetic Creativity from 1989 until July 1991. He was instrumental in planning the arbitration and distribution of the foundation's first meeting in May 1990 at the Marriott Hotel in Cairo, working with the Cairo-based Modern Literature Association.

Additionally, there is an Isa Al Shayji who worked as an editor at Al Ayam newspaper in Bahrain, facing criticism from Islamist groups in 2006. The International Federation of Journalists intervened on his behalf, with their Secretary General condemning what they called 'orchestrated bullying of a respected journalist.' The connections between these Al Shayji family members across Kuwait and Bahrain suggest a family with regional influence in politics, culture, and media.
data.description: The Al Shayji family is a Kuwaiti family known for their involvement in politics, culture, and media, with documented members serving in Kuwait's National Assembly and cultural institutions.
data.modernStatus: The family maintains political presence in Kuwait through Abdulaziz Al-Shayji's role in the National Assembly and has historical involvement in major cultural institutions like the Al-Babtain Foundation for Poetic Creativity.

data.history: Muhammad Taqi Misbah Yazdi Giwachi (1935-2021) was an Iranian Shia scholar whose family name reveals occupational origins - 'Giwachi' indicates his ancestors were producers of traditional footwear called Giwah. Born in Yazd, he completed primary education there before entering religious seminaries, first at Khān School and Shāfīʿiya School in his hometown, then briefly at Najaf's Hindi School in Iraq (1950), and finally at Qom Seminary where he studied under prominent figures including Ayatollah Khomeini from 1952-1960. His educational journey took him from traditional craft origins to becoming one of Iran's most influential conservative clerics. After graduating in 1960, he became politically active following the 1963 demonstrations against the Shah, though he withdrew from politics before Khomeini's 1964 exile to focus on theological work. He co-established the influential Haghani School in Qom with Mohammad Beheshti to train future Islamic Republic cadres. Following the 1979 revolution, Mesbah Yazdi emerged as a powerful conservative voice, serving in the Assembly of Experts from 1991-2016 and becoming known as the most conservative clerical oligarch in Qom. He was a strong advocate for absolute velayat-e faqih (guardianship of the Islamic jurist) and opposed democratic reforms, supporting Mahmoud Ahmadinejad's presidency while maintaining significant influence through his students in administrative and security posts.
data.description: The Al Mesbah family traces to Iranian Shia scholar Muhammad Taqi Misbah Yazdi (1935-2021), whose surname 'Giwachi' indicates ancestors who were traditional footwear craftsmen from Yazd. He became one of Iran's most influential conservative clerics and political theorists.
data.originStory: The family's occupational surname 'Giwachi' indicates their ancestors were producers of traditional footwear called Giwah, suggesting artisan origins in the city of Yazd, Iran.
data.modernStatus: The family gained prominence through Muhammad Taqi Misbah Yazdi's religious and political influence in the Islamic Republic of Iran. His students occupy sensitive administrative and security posts, and he served as director of the Imam Khomeini Education and Research Institute in Qom until his death in 2021.
data.nameEtymology: Giwachi is an occupational surname indicating ancestors who produced traditional footwear called Giwah. The family later adopted 'Mesbah' (meaning lamp or light in Arabic) as their scholarly name.

data.history: The Al Fahad family is a prominent branch of Kuwait's ruling House of Al-Sabah. The most notable figure is Sheikh Fahad Al-Ahmed Al-Jaber Al-Sabah (1945-1990), son of Ahmad Al-Jaber Al-Sabah and a Baloch woman named Fatima Mohammed Albalooshi. This indicates mixed Arab-Baloch heritage within this particular branch. Sheikh Fahad became a significant military officer and international sports administrator, founding the Asian Handball Federation and Kuwait Olympic Committee. He served in various military commands including the 2nd Commando Battalion of the 25th Commando Brigade and participated in the 1967 Six-Day War on the Egyptian front as part of the Yarmouk Brigade. Notably, he was also a member of the Palestinian group Fatah when it was headquartered in Jordan and later Lebanon, demonstrating the family's involvement in broader Arab political movements. His career culminated tragically when he was killed by Iraqi forces at Dasman Palace on August 2, 1990, during the first day of Iraq's invasion of Kuwait. His legacy continued through his son Ahmad Al-Fahad Al-Ahmed Al-Sabah (born 1963), who followed in his father's footsteps in both sports administration and government service, serving as Kuwait's Minister of Defense and heading various Olympic organizations, though his career has been marked by significant controversies including fraud convictions and corruption allegations.
data.description: Al Fahad is a branch of Kuwait's ruling House of Al-Sabah, notable for producing influential sports administrators and government officials, with mixed Arab-Baloch heritage through matrilineal descent.
data.originStory: The Al Fahad branch originates from the broader House of Al-Sabah, Kuwait's ruling family, which itself comes from the Bani Utbah confederation. The specific Al Fahad lineage shows mixed heritage through Sheikh Fahad Al-Ahmed's mother, Fatima Mohammed Albalooshi, who was Baloch.
data.modernStatus: The Al Fahad branch remains influential in Kuwaiti politics and international sports administration, though recent generations have faced significant legal challenges and corruption allegations in both domestic and international courts.

data.history: The Al Madhoun family appears to be a Palestinian family with documented members in journalism and media. Based on the research, Rabai al-Madhoun was born in 1945 in the village of al-Majdal in Palestine, near Ashkelon. His family was displaced during the 1948 Palestinian expulsion and flight, and he grew up in the Khan Younis refugee camp in the Gaza Strip. The family name appears in contemporary contexts related to Palestinian journalism and humanitarian work, with Ahmed al-Madhoun mentioned as an Egyptian journalist who was among the first to receive internet access through eSIMs during the Gaza conflict, and Ahmed Al-Madhoun listed as a paramedic who was killed while attempting to rescue Hind Rajab in 2024. The research also references Samih Madhoun, who died in 2007 and was described as a senior leader of the Al-Aqsa Martyrs Brigade affiliated with Fatah, though the exact family connections are not explicitly confirmed in the available sources.
data.description: A Palestinian family with documented presence in journalism, media, and humanitarian work, with members displaced from historical Palestine and currently active in various professional fields across the Middle East.
data.originStory: The family originated from the village of al-Majdal in Palestine, near Ashkelon. They were among the Palestinians displaced during the 1948 Palestinian expulsion and flight, with at least one documented family line settling in the Khan Younis refugee camp in Gaza.
data.modernStatus: Family members are active in journalism, media, and humanitarian fields, with notable figures working for international publications and organizations across the Middle East and Europe.

data.history: The Al Jassar family name appears in connection with a significant criminal case in Iraq during the 2000s. Ali Lutfi Jassar al-Rawi, also known as Abu Rasha, was an Iraqi architect from Baghdad who became involved in the Margaret Hassan kidnapping and murder case in 2004. Hassan was an Irish aid worker who had lived in Iraq for decades and worked with CARE International. In 2008, Jassar contacted the British Embassy in Baghdad attempting to extort 1 million dollars in return for disclosing the location of Hassan's body, reportedly having knowledge of intimate details about Hassan known only to her closest relatives and friends. On June 2, 2009, Baghdad's Central Criminal Court sentenced Jassar to life imprisonment for being involved in Hassan's abduction and murder, and for attempting to blackmail the British Embassy. However, in July 2010, it was discovered that Jassar had escaped from Baghdad central prison (formerly Abu Ghraib) during riots in September 2009, with Iraq's deputy justice minister confirming that people had facilitated his escape.
data.description: Al Jassar is documented as a family name associated with an Iraqi architect involved in a high-profile kidnapping case in Baghdad during the 2000s.

data.history: Al Baraka appears in this historical context as the maternal lineage name connected to Baraka Al Yamaniyah, a Yemeni woman of African descent who was a concubine of King Abdulaziz (Ibn Saud), the founder of Saudi Arabia. Baraka Al Yamaniyah was the mother of Prince Muqrin bin Abdulaziz, who became the 35th son of King Abdulaziz and briefly served as Crown Prince of Saudi Arabia from January to April 2015. She died in Riyadh on 22 August 2018. The research provides no information about Al Baraka as an independent family lineage, tribal origin, or business entity separate from this maternal connection to the Saudi royal family. The name appears solely in the context of Prince Muqrin's mother, with no details about her family background, tribal affiliations, or how her lineage came to be known as 'Al Baraka.' While Prince Muqrin had significant political and business roles, including serving as intelligence chief and founding the Hail Agricultural Development Company, the research contains no information about Al Baraka as a distinct family entity with its own history, businesses, or genealogical traditions.
data.description: Al Baraka appears in the research only as the maternal lineage name of Baraka Al Yamaniyah, a Yemeni woman of African descent who was King Abdulaziz's concubine and mother of Prince Muqrin bin Abdulaziz. No information is provided about Al Baraka as an independent family entity.

data.history: The Al Madani family descends from the Shikh Hasan Al Madani section of the Al Qureash, a highly respected and authoritative tribal federation. Their reign as rulers of Lavar Shikh began in 1087 when Sheikh Mohamad bin Sheikh Rashed bin Sheikh Mostafa Al Madani and around 1088 tribesmen moved from Medina. The family appears to have historical connections to the Arabian Peninsula, specifically the holy city of Medina, from where their migration originated in the late 11th century. The research indicates they established themselves as rulers in the Lavar Shikh region, though the specific geographical location and extent of their rule requires further clarification from historical sources.
data.description: The Al Madani family is a Gulf Arab family that descends from the Shikh Hasan Al Madani section of the Al Qureash tribal federation, with origins tracing back to Medina and a migration in 1087 CE.
data.originStory: According to historical records, the Al Madani family's reign as rulers began in 1087 when Sheikh Mohamad bin Sheikh Rashed bin Sheikh Mostafa Al Madani led approximately 1088 tribesmen in a migration from Medina to establish rule over Lavar Shikh.
