data.history: The Al Qasimi (also spelled Al Qassimi or Al Qassemi) is an Arab dynasty and tribe that rules Sharjah and Ras Al Khaimah, forming two of the seven emirates of the United Arab Emirates. They are one of the longest reigning royal families in the Arabian peninsula. The Qawasim tribe from which the dynasty originates are Huwala, their ancestors migrating and keeping connections between the Arabian Peninsula and Persia, particularly maintaining their rulership over the town of Lengeh on the coast of Hormozgan province. The Qawasim tribe itself is of Hashemite origin, descending from Husayn ibn Ali, through their eponymous ancestor Al Qasim bin Idris bin Ja'far al-Zaki. During the 18th century, embracing the ideals of Muhammad ibn 'Abd al-Wahhab's Muwahhidun movement, the Qawasim became a close ally of the Emirate of Diriyah. By the early 19th century, the Qawasim emerged as a maritime power based both in Ras Al Khaimah on the southern shore of the Persian Gulf and Qishm, Bandar Abbas and Lingeh on the northern shore. Their control of trade in the Persian Gulf led to conflicts with the British-allied Omani Empire and eventually with Britain itself, resulting in the Qawasim being labeled as 'pirates' by the British. This led to two major British military campaigns against them in 1809 and 1819, culminating in the General Maritime Treaty of 1820.
data.description: The Al Qasimi is an Arab dynasty and tribe of Hashemite origin that rules Sharjah and Ras Al Khaimah in the UAE. They are one of the longest reigning royal families in the Arabian peninsula with historical maritime power in the Persian Gulf.
data.originStory: The Qawasim tribe itself is of Hashemite origin, descending from Husayn ibn Ali, through their eponymous ancestor Al Qasim bin Idris bin Ja'far al-Zaki. The Qawasim tribe from which dynasty originates are Huwala, their ancestors migrating and keeping connections between the Arabian Peninsula and Persia, particularly maintaining their rulership over the town of Lengeh on the coast of Hormozgan province.
data.modernStatus: Current rulers of Sharjah (Sultan bin Muhammad Al-Qasimi) and Ras Al Khaimah (Saud bin Saqr Al Qasimi) emirates in the UAE. Sultan Sooud Al-Qassemi is a prominent member known as an educator, art collector, and founder of the Barjeel Art Foundation, as well as Chairman of Barjeel Geojit Securities.

data.history: The research reveals multiple distinct Al Khalaf families across the Gulf region with no established connection to each other. The House of Khalifa (Al Khalifa) represents the most prominent branch, ruling Bahrain since 1783 when Ahmed bin Muhammad bin Khalifa defeated Nasr Al-Madhkur and established control over the archipelago following the Bani Utbah invasion. The Al Khalifa belong to the Anizah tribe and profess Sunni Islam, having migrated from Najd in central Arabia to Kuwait, then ruled Al Zubarah in Qatar before settling in Bahrain in the early 17th century as part of the Utub alliance. After Ahmed's death in 1796, his sons Salman and Abdulla co-ruled Bahrain as feudal estates, leading to a split into Al-Salman and Al-Abdulla branches that engaged in conflict between 1842-1846, with the Al-Salman branch ultimately victorious. Today, Salman's descendants rule Bahrain while Abdulla's descendants live in Qatar. Other Al Khalaf families mentioned in the research include Khalaf Al Habtoor, a UAE-based businessman and Chairman of Al Habtoor Group who established a charitable foundation in 2013, and various historical figures like Palestinian militant leader Salah Khalaf (Abu Iyad) and ancient Arabian figures like Umayya ibn Khalaf, showing the diversity of families bearing this name across different regions and time periods.
data.description: Multiple distinct Gulf families bearing the Al Khalaf name, most prominently the ruling Al Khalifa dynasty of Bahrain since 1783, with origins in the Anizah tribe of Najd.
data.originStory: The Al Khalifa originated from the Anizah tribe in Najd, central Arabia, and joined the Utub alliance which migrated to Kuwait, then ruled Al Zubarah in Qatar before settling in Bahrain in the early 17th century.
data.modernStatus: The Al Khalifa continue as the ruling family of Bahrain under King Hamad bin Isa Al Khalifa, with family members holding key government positions including Prime Minister and multiple cabinet posts. Other Al Khalaf families operate successful businesses across the Gulf.

data.history: The Al Najjar family name derives from the Arabic "Banu Najjar" (بَنُو نَجَّار), meaning "sons of the carpenter." This designation refers to several unrelated historical and modern-day tribes throughout the Arab world, with the most historically significant being the Banu Najjar of Medina mentioned in the Charter of Medina. The Medinan Banu Najjar were the maternal clan of Prophet Muhammad's grandfather Abdul-Muttalib and were listed by Islamic historians like Ibn Hajar al-Asqalani and al-Tabari as a clan of the large Banu Khazraj tribe. Their ancestor was identified as Taymallah ibn Thalabah ibn Amr ibn al-Khazraj, and they had at least three sub-clans. Before Islam, they practiced traditional Arab polytheism and owned idols named Samul, Husa, and at-Tamm, which were destroyed after their conversion to Islam. The Prophet's Mosque was later built in their tribal garden, and Muhammad initially settled with them when he emigrated from Mecca to Medina. Notable figures from this historical tribe include Abu Ayyub al-Ansari (died c. 674), who served as Muhammad's standard-bearer and later became governor of Medina during Ali ibn Abi Talib's caliphate, and Zayd ibn Thabit, a prominent companion of the Prophet.
data.description: Al Najjar is a surname meaning "sons of the carpenter" that refers to several unrelated Arab tribes throughout history, most notably the Banu Najjar of Medina who were maternal relatives of Prophet Muhammad's grandfather and early supporters of Islam.
data.originStory: The name Al Najjar derives from the Arabic term for carpenter (najjar), indicating ancestral connection to carpentry or woodworking trades. The most significant historical Banu Najjar were descended from Taymallah ibn Thalabah ibn Amr ibn al-Khazraj in pre-Islamic Medina.
data.modernStatus: Contemporary Al Najjar families exist throughout the Arab world as separate lineages. The research mentions modern individuals like Nahla Al-Najjar (wife of Sami Al-Arian) and Mazen Al-Najjar, but provides no information about organized family business or political activities in the Gulf region.
data.nameEtymology: Al Najjar means "sons of the carpenter" in Arabic, from the root word najjar meaning carpenter or woodworker

data.history: The research provided contains information about several individuals and entities with "Shaheen" or related names, but none represent a GCC family called "Al Shaheen" with documented genealogical, business, or tribal lineage. The research includes American politician Jeanne Shaheen (née Bowers), Pakistani cricketer Shaheen Afridi from the Zakhakhel Afridi tribe, and various other individuals with the name Shaheen/Shahin across different regions. The name "Shahin" is documented as a Persian adjective meaning "majestic" or "kingly" and refers to a type of peregrine falcon, derived from "Shah" (king) and the suffix "īn" meaning "having the qualities of." However, no specific Al Shaheen family from the Gulf Cooperation Council region is documented in the provided research materials. Without concrete evidence of a GCC-based Al Shaheen family's origins, migration patterns, or historical development, no verified family history can be established.

data.history: The research provides information about several distinct Khalil families and individuals, but no unified Gulf family lineage. The Al-Khalil family mentioned in Wikipedia is described as an old Arabic tribe living in Jammain village in the Nablus district of the West Bank, belonging to the mother tribe of Al Zeitawi. A fraction of this family migrated and settled in Egypt. The Al Zeitawi tribe is described as arriving in Palestine from Mecca and believed to be direct descendants of Muhammad. However, this appears to be a Palestinian family rather than a GCC-based lineage. Other notable Khalils mentioned include Khalil Esfandiary-Bakhtiary, an Iranian diplomat and father of the Shah's second wife, and Kahlil Gibran, the Lebanese-American writer and poet. Pakistani singer Kaifi Khalil represents a modern Baloch musician from Karachi. The research does not establish a coherent Gulf family genealogy or business history for a unified Khalil family in the GCC region.
data.description: Multiple distinct Khalil families and individuals are documented across different regions, including a Palestinian Al-Khalil tribe, Iranian nobility, Lebanese-American literary figures, and Pakistani Baloch musicians, but no unified GCC family lineage is established in the research.
data.originStory: The Palestinian Al-Khalil family belongs to the Al Zeitawi tribe, which reportedly arrived in Palestine from Mecca and is believed to be direct descendants of Muhammad, though this appears to be folk tradition rather than documented genealogy.

data.history: The research provided focuses on two distinct historical figures named Khalid, neither of which represents a Gulf family genealogy. The primary subject is Khalid bin Abdulaziz Al Saud (1913-1982), who served as King of Saudi Arabia from 1975-1982. Born in Qasr Al Hukm, Riyadh, he was the fifth son of King Abdulaziz, founder of the Kingdom of Saudi Arabia. His mother was Al Jawhara bint Musaed from the Al Jiluwi clan, making him a member of the Al Saud ruling family rather than a separate family lineage. The second Khalid mentioned is Khalid ibn al-Walid (died 642 CE), a 7th-century Arab military commander known as 'Sword of God,' who belonged to the Banu Makhzum clan of the Quraysh tribe in Mecca. This historical research does not contain information about a Gulf family named Khalid with business or tribal genealogy separate from the Saudi royal family. The documentation focuses entirely on these two historical figures rather than family lineage, migration patterns, or business development in the modern Gulf context.
data.description: The research covers two historical figures named Khalid: King Khalid bin Abdulaziz Al Saud of Saudi Arabia (1913-1982) and the 7th-century military commander Khalid ibn al-Walid, but contains no information about a Gulf family genealogy.

data.history: The Adel family name derives from ancient European origins meaning 'noble' or 'nobility', evolving from proto-Germanic words meaning 'noble' and 'kin'. The name is found globally with over 15,000 instances as a given name and over 4,000 as a family name across 55 countries. It is most prominent in Nordic countries, the United States, United Kingdom, France, Kazakhstan and Russia. The earliest recorded individuals with this name include Princess Adel of Liège (born c. 632 AD) and King Adel of Sweden (born c. 572 AD). In the Arabian Gulf context, one notable figure is Abdulla Adel Fakhro, a Bahraini businessman and politician from a prominent Bahraini business family who currently serves as Minister of Industry and Commerce. The research also identifies two other significant figures using 'Adel' - Gholam-Ali Haddad-Adel, an Iranian politician, and Saif al-Adel, an Egyptian militant leader - though these appear to be unrelated to any Gulf family lineage.
data.description: Adel is primarily a European-origin name meaning 'noble' that appears globally, including among some prominent Gulf business and political families, though specific tribal or family connections are not established in the research.
data.originStory: The name Adel derives from Old Dutch 'ōþil', Old German 'adal', Old Norse 'aðal', Old French 'œ̄ðel', and Old English 'æðel' by evolution of proto-Germanic 'aþalą' meaning 'noble' and 'kin' and 'ōþilą' meaning 'inheritance' and 'rule of the land'.
data.modernStatus: In the Gulf context, the research identifies Abdulla Adel Fakhro as a prominent Bahraini businessman and politician serving as Minister of Industry and Commerce, member of a prominent Bahraini business family with 20 years experience in the Fakhro Group business empire.

data.history: The Gupta family originated from the Indian state of Uttar Pradesh and migrated to South Africa in 1993 to establish Sahara Computers, initially operating as a technology trading business. The family was based at the Sahara Estate in Saxonwold, Johannesburg, comprising at least four mansions, until 2016 when they relocated to Dubai, United Arab Emirates. The family built a substantial business empire spanning multiple industries including information technology, mining, media, and energy through their holding company Oakbay Investments. Their influence grew significantly through close ties to former South African President Jacob Zuma, with allegations of state capture and corruption leading to international sanctions and investigations. By 2016, Atul Gupta had become the seventh-wealthiest person in South Africa with an estimated net worth of R10.7 billion, but mounting legal pressures forced the family to flee South Africa. The family has since been the subject of criminal investigations in multiple countries, with Atul and Rajesh Gupta facing fraud and money laundering charges, though extradition attempts from the UAE to South Africa failed in 2022-2023.
data.description: A wealthy Indian business family who migrated from Uttar Pradesh to South Africa in 1993, becoming central figures in corruption scandals involving former President Jacob Zuma and facing international sanctions and criminal charges.
data.originStory: The family originated from the Indian state of Uttar Pradesh and migrated to South Africa in 1993 to establish their first business venture, Sahara Computers.
data.modernStatus: The family operates internationally through various business entities but faces ongoing criminal investigations, sanctions from multiple countries, and extradition requests. They currently reside in Dubai, UAE, with business interests severely curtailed due to legal challenges.

data.history: The Agarwal family name appears prominently in Indian business, entertainment, and social sectors, with documented presence across multiple industries and regions. Ganga Bhishen Agarwal (1908-1985), known as Haldiram Ji, founded Haldiram Snacks Food in Bikaner, Rajasthan in 1937, establishing one of India's leading snack food brands. The family business began with his father's bhujia shop, and Ganga Bhishen's innovative approach using moth ki dal instead of besan made their products popular. By the 1980s, Haldiram's expanded to major cities including Delhi, Nagpur, and Kolkata, eventually dividing into three separate companies run by different Agarwal family branches. The business now operates globally with products available in over 80 countries. In entertainment, the name appears in various television productions, with fictional Agarwal families featured in popular shows like 'Kahaani Ghar Ghar Kii' and 'Mann Atisundar', representing typical Marwari joint family structures.
data.description: The Agarwal name is associated with successful Indian business ventures, particularly in the food industry with Haldiram's snacks empire, and appears prominently in Indian entertainment media representing traditional joint family values.
data.modernStatus: The Agarwal business empire includes Haldiram's snack food companies operating globally, with presence in over 80 countries. Multiple family branches operate separate companies in Kolkata, Nagpur, and Delhi. Contemporary members are involved in technology, social activism, and entertainment industries.

data.history: The Malhotra family is a prominent Indian film family in Hindi cinema, with Krishna Malhotra being a key connecting figure. Krishna Malhotra, sister of actors Prem Nath, Rajendra Nath, and Narendra Nath, married legendary actor Raj Kapoor of the influential Kapoor family, thereby establishing significant connections within Bollywood's founding dynasties. The family includes Prem Nath, a notable actor who was married to actress Bina Rai, and their son Prem Krishen who continued the acting tradition. Another sister, Uma Malhotra, married actor Prem Chopra, further expanding the family's influence in the film industry. The Malhotra family's integration with the Kapoor dynasty through Krishna's marriage to Raj Kapoor represents one of the most significant alliances between two major Bollywood families, contributing to the establishment of what became known as the 'First Family of Bollywood.'
data.description: A prominent Indian film family connected to Bollywood through marriages with the Kapoor family and active participation in Hindi cinema through multiple generations.
data.modernStatus: The family maintains connections to the Hindi film industry through their historical ties to the Kapoor family dynasty and continued presence in Bollywood circles.

data.history: The Al Salimi family is documented through one notable historical figure, Nur al-Din al-Salimi (1869–1914), who was an Omani Ibadi scholar. The research provides no information about the family's origins, tribal affiliation, or how they came to their current location. Al-Salimi lived during the late 19th and early 20th centuries in Oman, during a period when the region was under various forms of external influence and internal tribal dynamics. The family appears to have had scholarly traditions within the Ibadi Islamic tradition, which is the dominant religious sect in Oman. Beyond this single historical figure, no additional information about the Al Salimi family's broader history, business activities, or political involvement is available in the provided research materials.
data.description: An Omani family known for producing at least one notable Ibadi Islamic scholar in the early 20th century, with no documented information about their origins or broader family history.

data.history: The Al Mahri (المهريون) are an Arab ethnographic group primarily inhabiting South Arabia, especially in the Al-Mahra Governorate in Yemen and the island of Socotra. They are descended from Mahra ibn Haydan ibn Amr ibn al-Hafi Quda'a ibn Malik ibn Amr ibn Murra ibn Zayd ibn Malik ibn Ḥimyar, who was from Quda'a. The Mahri established the Mahra Sultanate, which lasted for five centuries until its abolition in 1967 upon the founding of the People's Republic of South Yemen. The sultanate was consistently ruled by the Al-Mahri dynasty from 1750 to 1967, with the last reigning sultan being Isa bin Ali al-Afrar al-Mahri. During the early years of Islam, the Mahri tribe played a significant role in the Islamic conquests of North Africa and Spain under Amr ibn al-As, participating in battles including Heliopolis and Nikiou in Egypt in 646. They were granted lands in the newly occupied territories, including the area of Jabal Yashkur and what became known as Hayy Al-Mehri (the Mahri Quarter) in Fustat. In 1886, the Sultanate came under the British-ruled Aden Protectorate, and later under the Protectorate of South Arabia before its final abolition in 1967.
data.description: The Al Mahri are an Arab tribe primarily inhabiting the Al-Mahra Governorate in Yemen and Socotra island, known for their unique Mahri language and their historical Mahra Sultanate which lasted five centuries until 1967.
data.originStory: The Mahri are descended from Mahra ibn Haydan ibn Amr ibn al-Hafi Quda'a ibn Malik ibn Amr ibn Murra ibn Zayd ibn Malik ibn Ḥimyar, who was from Quda'a.
data.modernStatus: The Al Mahri are distributed across multiple countries in the Arabian Peninsula including Yemen, Oman, Kuwait, Saudi Arabia, and the UAE. According to Ethnologue, there are around 115,200 total Mahri speakers, with 50,000 in Yemen, 50,800 in Oman, 14,400 in Kuwait, and around 20,000 in Saudi Arabia.
data.nameEtymology: They are named after Mahra bin Haydan

data.history: The Al Mamari family is an Omani family with documented presence in the Sultanate of Oman. The research indicates a notable historical connection through Abu Mansur Mamari, a Persian-era scholar who supervised the creation of the Shahnameh of Abu Mansur in 957 CE. This work was a New Persian prose text created by Zoroastrian scholars and dihqans, which served as a primary source for Ferdowsi's later Shahnameh. In contemporary times, the Al Mamari family has produced individuals active in politics, human rights, and sports, demonstrating their continued engagement in Omani public life. The family name appears in various contexts within Oman, from political activism to athletic achievement, suggesting they are an established part of Omani society.
data.description: An Omani family with historical scholarly connections and contemporary involvement in politics, human rights activism, and sports within the Sultanate of Oman.
data.modernStatus: The Al Mamari family maintains an active presence in Omani public life, with members involved in politics, human rights advocacy, and sports representation for Qatar and Oman.

data.history: The research provided does not contain information about the Al Farsi family specifically. While the historical figure Salman al-Farsi (Salman the Persian) is documented as a prominent companion of Prophet Muhammad who was originally from Persia (born Ruzbeh Khoshnudan, likely between 568-570 AD), there is no evidence connecting him to a modern Gulf family named Al Farsi. The research also mentions Yasmine Pahlavi (née Etemad-Amini) who married into the Iranian royal family, but again provides no connection to a Gulf-based Al Farsi family lineage. The Persian language information confirms the geographical and cultural context of Fars province in Iran, but does not establish any specific family genealogy. Without direct evidence linking these historical references to a contemporary Gulf family, no verified family history can be established.
data.description: No verified information about an Al Farsi family in the GCC context is available in the provided research materials.

data.history: The Al Shueili family appears to be an Omani family with established presence in the Sultanate's governmental and professional sectors. Based on available records, family members have achieved significant positions in both public service and private industry. Khalfan bin Saeed bin Mubarak al-Shueili represents the contemporary generation, holding advanced degrees from international universities including Drexel University and the University of Glasgow, and serving in high-level government positions since 2020. The family name also appears in Omani media, with Abdullah bin Salim al Shueili serving as Editor-in-Chief of the Oman Daily Observer since 2013. Given that Oman is predominantly Ibadi Muslim, and the family's integration into government service, they likely follow the Ibadi sect of Islam, which has been the dominant religious tradition in Oman for over a millennium and forms the foundation of the country's political and social structure.
data.description: The Al Shueili family is an established Omani family with members holding significant positions in government service and media, representing the modern educated professional class of the Sultanate.

data.history: The Al Amri family name appears across multiple regions of the Arabian Peninsula and beyond, with documented presence in Saudi Arabia, Oman, and Tunisia. In Saudi Arabia, the family is notably represented by Othman Hadi Al Maqboul al-Amri from al-Namas Province, who was placed on Saudi Arabia's most wanted terrorist list in December 2003 before surrendering in June 2004. The research indicates he served as a Sergeant in the Saudi army and was married twice with five children, suggesting an established family presence in the region. In Oman, members of the Al Amri family have been recognized for their contributions to the country's development, with both Ahmed bin Mohammad bin Salim al Amri and Mohammad bin Musalim bin Saif al Amri receiving the Order of the Renaissance (Fourth Class) in 1973. The Tunisian branch is represented by actress Lamia Amri, born in 1972, who has had a notable career in theatre, television, and film before relocating to Lyon, France. The geographic distribution suggests the Al Amri name may represent multiple distinct family lines rather than a single tribal origin, though specific genealogical connections between these branches are not established in the available research.
data.description: The Al Amri family represents multiple documented lineages across Saudi Arabia, Oman, and Tunisia, with members achieving recognition in military service, civil contributions, and entertainment industries.
data.modernStatus: Members of Al Amri families hold various positions across different countries, including civil service recognition in Oman and entertainment industry success in Tunisia, with some members having relocated to France

data.history: Based on the available research, Dr Jamil bin Darwish bin Jamil Al-Shaqsi is the only confirmed member of the Al Shaqsi family found in the documentation. He currently serves as a member of the Board of Directors of the Central Bank of Oman, as appointed by Royal Decree 4/2025. The Central Bank of Oman was established in December 1974 and began operations on 1 April 1975, replacing the Oman Currency Board as the principal currency authority in Oman. In January 2025, the bank underwent significant structural reforms through a series of Royal Decrees, including Royal Decree 3/2025 which reorganized the bank's governance structure and granted it legal personality and financial and administrative independence. Dr Al-Shaqsi's appointment to this prestigious position indicates the family's involvement in Oman's financial sector and their recognition at the highest levels of government.

data.history: The Al Harasi (Harasis) are a tribal group primarily inhabiting the Jiddat al-Harasis plateau in south-central Oman. They are speakers of the Harsusi language, a critically endangered Modern South Arabian language with an estimated 600-1000 speakers as of recent surveys. The Harasis people were historically the only human inhabitants of the Jiddat al-Harasis, a stony desert region, which allowed their language and culture to develop in relative isolation for centuries. Their traditional way of life centered around pastoralism and they were particularly known for their expertise in desert survival and navigation. The discovery of oil in their territory and conservation projects for the reintroduced Arabian oryx herd have provided employment opportunities for Harasis men in recent decades. However, these economic changes have accelerated language shift toward Arabic and Mehri, as most Harasis children now attend Arabic-language schools and are literate in Arabic rather than their ancestral tongue. UNESCO has categorized Harsusi as 'definitely endangered' due to declining intergenerational transmission.
data.description: The Al Harasi are a small tribal group of south-central Oman who speak the critically endangered Harsusi language and traditionally inhabited the Jiddat al-Harasis desert plateau. They number 1-2000 people and have historically lived as pastoralists in one of Arabia's most isolated regions.
data.modernStatus: The Harasis continue to live in Jiddat al-Harasis but face significant cultural and linguistic pressures. Oil discovery and oryx conservation projects have created employment opportunities but accelerated Arabization. Their language is critically endangered with UNESCO classification as 'definitely endangered'.

data.history: The Al Rahbi family name appears in various contexts across the Arabian Peninsula and wider Islamic world, with notable bearers spanning different centuries and regions. Historical evidence shows individuals bearing the Al Rahbi name in diverse fields including poetry, Islamic scholarship, and modern detention cases. Saif al-Rahbi, born in 1956, is a contemporary Omani poet, essayist and writer who has gained recognition in Arabic literature. The name also appears in connection with Abdel Malik Ahmed Abdel Wahab Al Rahabi, a Yemeni who was held at Guantanamo Bay from 2001 to 2016 before being granted asylum in Montenegro. Additionally, the name has geographical connections, as evidenced by Al Rahbi Park in Baghdad's Adhamiyah district, which notably contains the tomb of the famous 9th-10th century Islamic scholar Muhammad ibn Jarir al-Tabari.
data.description: Al Rahbi is a family name found across the Arabian Peninsula and Islamic world, with documented bearers in Oman, Yemen, and historical connections to Iraq, spanning from medieval Islamic scholarship to contemporary literature and modern political contexts.
data.modernStatus: Contemporary Al Rahbi family members include notable literary figures such as Saif al-Rahbi, an Omani poet and writer born in 1956 who contributes to Arabic literature and cultural discourse.
data.nameEtymology: The name 'Rahbi' likely derives from the Arabic root related to 'rahb' meaning spacious or wide, though specific etymology is not confirmed in available sources.

data.history: Based on the archaeological research provided, there is no direct information about the Al Jahwari family's genealogy, origins, or tribal lineage. The research focuses entirely on the Samad al-Shan archaeological site in the Sharqiyah province of Oman, which dates to the Late Iron Age (c. 300 BCE to c. 300 CE). This site represents the Samad Period archaeological assemblage and contains graves and artifacts from an ancient population that practiced date farming and had a sedentary lifestyle. The research discusses the material culture, burial practices, and potential South Arabian origins of this ancient population, but makes no connection to any modern Gulf families or tribal lineages. The archaeological evidence suggests this ancient population may have been migrants from South Arabia, but their relationship to contemporary Omani families remains undocumented in the available research.
data.description: No verified information about the Al Jahwari family is present in the provided archaeological research on the Samad al-Shan site.

data.history: Based on the available research, Al Ruwaily appears to be a geographical family designation rather than a distinct tribal lineage, primarily associated with locations named Al Ruwais or variations thereof across the Gulf region. The name appears in multiple contexts - from the former name of Al Dhannah (previously called Al Ruwais) in Abu Dhabi's Western Region, which was transformed from a small fishing settlement into a major industrial complex centered around the Ruwais Refinery officially inaugurated in 1982, to Ar-Ruʼays (Al Ruwais) in northern Qatar, historically one of the most important fishing centers on the peninsula. The Qatari Al Ruwais has a more documented tribal history, with family traditions stating it was one of the initial destinations of the Al Thani family before they settled in Fuwayrit in the late 1700s, and was considered the capital of the Al Jalahma tribe, part of the Bani Utbah clans who migrated from Kuwait in the mid-18th century. British surveys from the 1820s recorded inhabitants as being from the Abookara (Al Kuwari) and Uttoobee (Bani Utbah) tribes, while later records mention the Sadah tribe as the main inhabitants, suggesting complex tribal movements and settlements rather than a single family origin.
data.description: Al Ruwaily appears to be a geographical designation associated with settlements named Al Ruwais across the UAE and Qatar, rather than a single distinct family lineage, with historical connections to various tribal groups including the Bani Utbah and Al Sadah.
data.modernStatus: Associated with major industrial and port developments in both UAE and Qatar, including the Ruwais industrial complex in Abu Dhabi and Al Ruwais Port in northern Qatar.
data.nameEtymology: Ar Ru'ays translates to 'small head' in Arabic, named because the town juts out into the sea relative to the land surrounding it

data.history: The Al Jarbou family appears in the research primarily in two distinct contexts - as Syrian Druze religious leaders and as part of the Al Aqilat trading community in Saudi Arabia's Al-Qassim Province. In Syria, the Jarbou family has held religious leadership positions within the Druze community for over three centuries in Jabal al-Arab (Jabal al-Druze). Youssef Jarbou became one of the spiritual leaders of the Syrian Druze community on December 20, 2012, following the death of his cousin and predecessor Hussein Jarbou, continuing this hereditary religious office. In Saudi Arabia, the Al Jarbou family is mentioned among the important families of the Aqilat - settled tribes and families from Najd, specifically from Al-Qassim Province, who were famous traders in the late 1850s and early 1900s. These Aqilat families, including Al Jarbou, were renowned for trading gold, Arabian horses, camels, clothing and food across the Arabian Peninsula, reaching Kuwait, Iraq, Jordan, and Syria. The research does not establish any direct connection between the Syrian Druze Jarbou religious leaders and the Saudi Arabian Al Jarbou trading family, suggesting these may be distinct family lines sharing the same surname.
data.description: Al Jarbou appears as both a Syrian Druze religious leadership family with over 300 years of spiritual authority in Jabal al-Arab, and as part of the Al Aqilat trading families from Al-Qassim Province, Saudi Arabia, active in regional commerce during the late 19th and early 20th centuries.
data.modernStatus: In Syria, Youssef Jarbou serves as one of three Druze spiritual leaders, wielding significant religious and social influence in Suwayda Governorate. During recent conflicts in July 2025, he initially cooperated with government ceasefire calls alongside Sheikh Hammoud al-Hinnawi, though later issued statements opposing the Syrian government and describing events as systematic ethnic cleansing.

data.history: Al Hithlain is a prominent Saudi Arabian tribal family that has played a significant role in the kingdom's political landscape through marriage alliances with the House of Saud. The family traces its lineage to tribal leaders including Rakan bin Hithlain and his ancestor Dhaydan bin Hithlain, who were leaders of the Al Ajman tribe. The most notable connection to the Saudi royal family is through Princess Fahda bint Falah Al Hithlain, who became the third wife of King Salman bin Abdulaziz Al Saud. This marriage alliance has positioned the Al Hithlain family at the center of Saudi political power, as Princess Fahda is the mother of Crown Prince Mohammed bin Salman, the current heir apparent and de facto ruler of Saudi Arabia. The family's influence extends through their children, including Prince Turki bin Salman, Prince Khalid bin Salman (the current Defense Minister), and other princes who hold significant positions in the Saudi government. According to U.S. intelligence reports mentioned in the research, Princess Fahda has been kept apart from King Salman for several years on the orders of their son Prince Mohammed bin Salman, indicating the complex family dynamics within the royal court.
data.description: A prominent Saudi tribal family connected to the Al Ajman tribe, most notably through Princess Fahda bint Falah Al Hithlain, third wife of King Salman and mother of Crown Prince Mohammed bin Salman.
data.originStory: The Al Hithlain family descends from tribal leaders of the Al Ajman tribe, with documented lineage through Dhaydan bin Hithlain and his descendant Rakan bin Hithlain, who held leadership positions within the tribal structure.
data.modernStatus: The family holds significant political influence through their connection to the Saudi royal family, particularly as the maternal family of Crown Prince Mohammed bin Salman and Defense Minister Prince Khalid bin Salman.

data.history: The Al Nasser family in the research represents multiple distinct lineages sharing the name 'Nasser' but with different origins and histories. The most prominent figure documented is Sheikha Moza bint Nasser Al-Missned, whose father Nasser bin Abdullah Al-Missned was a well-known opposition activist and former head of the Al-Muhannada confederation of Bani Hajer. Born in Qatar, Nasser bin Abdullah faced exile following imprisonment for political activities and defiance against the policies of the deposed emir Ahmad bin Ali Al Thani, only returning to Qatar with his family in 1977. His daughter Moza married Hamad bin Khalifa Al Thani the same year, becoming the mother of the current Emir of Qatar, Sheikh Tamim bin Hamad Al Thani. The family's political influence expanded significantly through this marriage alliance, with Moza becoming a major figure in Qatar's educational and social development initiatives. The research also mentions other unrelated 'Nasser' lineages, including Nasser bin Zayed Al Nahyan of the UAE's ruling family, and the Egyptian revolutionary leader Gamal Abdel Nasser, demonstrating that 'Al Nasser' represents multiple distinct family lines across the region rather than a single unified genealogical entity.
data.description: Multiple distinct families bearing the Al Nasser name, most notably the Qatari Al-Missned branch connected to the ruling Al Thani family through marriage and political influence.
data.originStory: The documented Al-Missned branch of Al Nasser traces to Nasser bin Abdullah Al-Missned, who served as head of the Al-Muhannada confederation of Bani Hajer and was born in Qatar before facing political exile.
data.modernStatus: The Al-Missned branch holds significant political influence in Qatar through marriage ties to the ruling Al Thani family, with Sheikha Moza serving as chair of Qatar Foundation and mother to the current Emir.

data.history: The Al Dulaimi family is part of the larger Dulaim tribe, the largest Sunni Arab tribe in Iraq with over three million members. The tribe's history extends back to pre-Islamic times, with some scholars believing they participated in the first Arab Conquests in the 7th century. Originally Bedouins living between Ramadi and Al-Qa'im, the Dulaimis began migrating eastward at the end of the 15th century, eventually reaching Fallujah and areas south of Baghdad. The tribe controlled vast territories along the Euphrates from below Al Hillah and southern Baghdad to Fallujah, Ramadi, al-Qaim, Samarra and Mosul. During the 18th century, they established what was effectively the Principality of Dulaim (Amirate Al-Dulaim), an almost autonomous state that controlled agricultural and pastoral areas along the Tigris and Euphrates rivers. The tribe engaged in numerous conflicts with Ottoman forces from 1790 to 1890 when they refused to pay taxes for nearly a century and a half. Following World War I, many Dulaim clans fought against British forces, particularly during the 1920 insurrection. During the modern Iraqi state period, Dulaimis played significant roles in founding institutions and comprised 20-30% of the Iraqi Army during Saddam's era.
data.description: Al Dulaimi is part of the Dulaim tribe, Iraq's largest Sunni Arab tribe with over three million members, historically controlling territories along the Euphrates and Tigris rivers and playing major roles in Iraqi politics and military.
data.originStory: The Dulaims are believed to have origins in pre-Islamic times, with some scholars suggesting they were part of the first Arab Conquests in the 7th century. They originally lived as Bedouins between Ramadi and Al-Qa'im before beginning eastward migration in the late 15th century.
data.modernStatus: Al Dulaimi family members have held prominent positions in modern Iraqi politics and academia, including Naziha al-Dulaimi as Iraq's first female minister and the Arab world's first woman cabinet minister, and Adnan al-Dulaimi as a leading Sunni politician and Islamic studies professor at Baghdad University.
data.nameEtymology: Al-Dulaimi indicates belonging to the Dulaim tribe. The surname literally means 'The one from Baghdad' in some contexts, though this appears to be a specific reference rather than the general tribal etymology.

migrationPath.description: Eastward migration from original Bedouin territories between Ramadi and Al-Qa'im to areas around Fallujah and south of Baghdad

data.history: Based on the available research, the Al Abdeh family appears to have connections to both Iranian tribal lineages and Lebanese political contexts. The name 'Abdeh' appears as a lineage within the Movri tribe of the Bakhtiari people in Iran, specifically listed among the notable lineages as 'Abdeh-wand.' This suggests potential Persian tribal origins within the broader Bakhtiari confederation. Additionally, individuals with the surname 'Abdeh' appear in Lebanese political contexts, particularly in relation to the 1982 kidnapping of Iranian diplomats, where an individual named 'Abdeh Raji' (known as 'Captain') was reportedly involved in commanding a checkpoint where Iranian diplomats were detained by Lebanese Phalange forces. The family's current status and geographic distribution remain unclear from the available research, though the name appears in both Iranian tribal genealogies and Lebanese political affairs during the 1980s.
data.description: A family name with documented connections to both Iranian Bakhtiari tribal lineages and Lebanese political contexts, though comprehensive genealogical details remain limited in available sources.
data.modernStatus: Status unclear from available research

data.history: Al Suwait is a branch of the Al-Dhafeer Bedouin Arab tribe that descends from Tayy. The Al-Dhafeer tribe's ancestral homes can be traced back to areas near Medina, and they genealogically came from Banu Lam. The tribe spread throughout Arabia through raiding other tribes and immigration as they did not have a proper settlement. Al Suwait belongs to the Ahl al-Buwait division of Al-Dhafeer, which originally received its name as a laqab (nickname) due to their habit of defending refugees. Most of the Al-Dhafeer tribe, including Al Suwait, now lives in Saudi Arabia and Kuwait, taking positions such as professors, politicians, scholars, doctors and engineers. In modern Kuwaiti politics, members of Al Suwait have been active as parliamentarians, with notable involvement in opposition politics during Kuwait's recent political gridlock period from 2020-2024.
data.description: Al Suwait is a branch of the Al-Dhafeer Bedouin Arab tribe that descends from Tayy and Banu Lam, with ancestral origins near Medina. They are part of the Ahl al-Buwait division and have been active in modern Kuwaiti politics.
data.originStory: The Al-Dhafeer tribe, from which Al Suwait descends, genealogically came from Banu Lam and descended from Tayy. Their ancestral homes can be traced back to areas near Medina, specifically from a place opposite Medina named al-Dhaghn. The tribe spread throughout Arabia through raiding other tribes and immigration as they did not have a proper settlement.
data.tribalOrigin: Al-Dhafeer (branch of Banu Lam, descended from Tayy)
data.modernStatus: Members of Al Suwait hold positions in Kuwait as politicians and parliamentarians. They have been active in Kuwaiti opposition politics, with Al-Suwait being one of the MPs who requested interpolation of Prime Minister Sabah Al-Khalid in January 2021 during Kuwait's political gridlock period.

data.history: The Al Hebsi (also known as Al Habsi) family traces its origins to the Habus tribe, which settled in the mountainous border regions between Ras Al Khaimah and northern areas around the early 19th century. From approximately the beginning of the 19th century, the Habus tribe populated the area near Jabal Qada'ah, establishing settlements on the eastern slopes of Jabal Hebs mountain. The tribe was organized into distinct tribal areas, with the Al Hebsi belonging to the Banī Sā'ad division, one of the main branches alongside Banī Huraymish. The villages of Ra's and Magam on the eastern slopes of Jabal Hebs became known as 'Deira Al-Hebsi' or 'Deira Al-Habsi,' marking them as the first settlements of the Habus tribe in this strategic border area. These settlements were positioned between the Shihuh tribal territories to the north of Wadi Bih and the Habus tribal areas to the south, including the important Banī Sā'ad tribal area where the Al Hebsi established their primary presence.
data.description: The Al Hebsi family originates from the Habus tribe's Banī Sā'ad division, establishing mountain settlements in Ras Al Khaimah around the early 19th century. Their ancestral villages of Ra's and Magam became known as Deira Al-Hebsi, representing the first Habus tribal settlements in the strategic border region between different tribal territories.
data.originStory: The Al Hebsi established their first settlements in the mountainous region of Ras Al Khaimah around the beginning of the 19th century as part of the Habus tribe's expansion into the area near Jabal Qada'ah. They belonged to the Banī Sā'ad tribal division and founded villages on the eastern slopes of Jabal Hebs, which became known as Deira Al-Hebsi, marking their role as the pioneering Habus settlers in this border region.
data.nameEtymology: The name Al Hebsi derives from Jabal Hebs (also spelled Jabal Hibs), the 980-meter mountain where their ancestral villages were established, with 'Hebsi' indicating association with or origin from the Hebs mountain area.

data.history: The Al Shamisi family appears to be an established Emirati family with documented presence in the UAE, particularly in Abu Dhabi. The most prominent contemporary figure is Captain Mohamed Juma Al Shamisi, born in September 1978 in Abu Dhabi, who has risen to become Group CEO of AD Ports Group, one of the UAE's major infrastructure companies. He joined Abu Dhabi Ports in 2008 and was appointed CEO in 2014, demonstrating the family's integration into the UAE's modern business elite. The family name also appears in various contexts including sports, with Ahmed Abdulla Al Shamisi as an Emirati footballer and Hamda Taryam Al Shamsi noted as a professional drag racer, philanthropist and businesswoman. The family's presence spans multiple emirates and sectors, indicating their establishment within UAE society. However, the research provides no information about the family's origins, tribal connections, or historical migration patterns to the UAE.
data.description: The Al Shamisi is an established Emirati family with notable presence in business and sports, most prominently represented by Captain Mohamed Juma Al Shamisi, CEO of AD Ports Group. The family appears integrated across multiple sectors of UAE society.
data.modernStatus: The Al Shamisi family maintains significant business prominence through Captain Mohamed Juma Al Shamisi's leadership of AD Ports Group, a major UAE infrastructure company publicly listed on Abu Dhabi Securities Exchange since 2022. Family members are also represented in sports and other professional sectors.

data.history: The Al Murrah are an Arab tribe descended from the Banu Yam tribe, primarily residing in Qatar, Saudi Arabia, United Arab Emirates, Kuwait and Bahrain. Historically, they were a tribe of camel-herding nomads who controlled and travelled through vast areas of the Arabian Peninsula, covering as much as 3,000 kilometres annually. The Saudi branch comprises seven clans with approximately 15,000 individuals. In Qatar, the Al Murrah historically made up a large proportion of the ethnic population. In 1885, a number of Al Murrah tribespeople, along with several members from the Bani Hajer tribe, renounced their allegiance to Qatar and departed from the country, joining with the Ajman tribe in conflicts against Qatari forces. A majority of Al Murrah members were strong supporters of Sheikh Khalifa Al Thani, the former Emir of Qatar who was deposed in 1995. Eight months after the coup, 119 Al Murrah members attempted to overthrow the new Emir Sheikh Hamad Al Thani but failed. In February 2000, 19 of the alleged perpetrators were sentenced to death and 33 to life in prison, though none of those sentenced to death were executed. In 2017, Qatar revoked the citizenship of the sheikh and 54 tribespeople, and Al Murrah tribespeople were not allowed to run for the 2021 Qatari general election, which led to protests.
data.description: The Al Murrah are an Arab tribe descended from Banu Yam, historically camel-herding nomads who controlled vast areas of the Arabian Peninsula. They have faced political tensions in Qatar, particularly regarding their support for the former Emir Sheikh Khalifa Al Thani.
data.originStory: The Al Murrah are descended from the Banu Yam tribe and were historically camel-herding nomads who travelled through vast areas of the Arabian Peninsula.
data.modernStatus: The Al Murrah maintain presence across Gulf states including Qatar, Saudi Arabia, UAE, Kuwait and Bahrain. In Qatar, they have faced political restrictions including citizenship revocation in 2017 and exclusion from the 2021 elections due to their historical opposition to the current ruling family.

data.history: The Al Arrayedh family is a prominent Bahraini family with deep roots in the country's legal and political establishment. The most notable member is Jawad Salem Al Arrayed (born 1941), who has served in numerous high-ranking government positions throughout his career. Born in Manama in 1940, he obtained degrees in English literature from Cairo University and law from Leeds University, positioning himself as one of the highest-ranking Bahrainis educated and trained as a lawyer. His career began as a public prosecutor from 1969 to 1971, after which he held various ministerial positions including labor and social affairs minister (1971), minister of state for cabinet affairs (1972-1982), health minister (1982-1995), and minister of state for municipalities and environmental affairs (1995). Al Arrayed played a crucial role in Bahrain's legal battle with Qatar over the Hawar Islands, advising Isa bin Salman Al Khalifa and his government on the international dispute. He submitted to the International Court of Justice that Bahrain held sovereignty over the Hawar Islands, including Janan and Hadd Janan, and Bahrain ultimately prevailed in this case. In 2006, he was appointed Deputy Prime Minister, replacing a member of the royal family, Abdullah bin Khalid Al Khalifa, following the victory of the Shia Islamist party Al Wefaq in parliamentary elections. He also served as Minister of Justice from 2002 to 2005 and has been president of the ministerial committee for legal affairs.
data.description: A prominent Bahraini family known for their significant contributions to the country's legal and political establishment, particularly through Jawad Salem Al Arrayed's extensive government service.
data.modernStatus: The Al Arrayedh family maintains high political status in Bahrain, with Jawad Al Arrayed serving as President of the Ministerial Committee for Legal Affairs and former Deputy Prime Minister. The family is deeply embedded in Bahrain's legal and governmental institutions.

data.history: The Al Hazmi (الحازمي) family is a Saudi Arabian family with documented presence in Mecca. Based on available records, Muhammad Salim al-Hazmi worked as a grocer in Mecca and had at least two sons: Nawaf al-Hazmi (born 1976) and Salem al-Hazmi (born 1981). The family name appears to be derived from the Arabic root meaning 'decisive' or 'resolute.' The family gained international notoriety when both Nawaf and Salem al-Hazmi participated in the September 11, 2001 terrorist attacks as hijackers of American Airlines Flight 77, which crashed into the Pentagon. Prior to this, both brothers had been involved with al-Qaeda and had fighting experience in various conflicts including Bosnia, Afghanistan, and possibly Chechnya during the 1990s.
data.description: A Saudi Arabian family from Mecca, primarily known through the documented activities of Muhammad Salim al-Hazmi and his two sons who became involved in international terrorism in the early 2000s.
data.modernStatus: The family's current status is largely unknown following the deaths of the two documented sons in the September 11 attacks and the international scrutiny that followed.
data.nameEtymology: Al-Hazmi (الحازمي) appears to be derived from Arabic, though the specific etymological meaning is not provided in the research materials.

data.history: The Al Saud Al Murrah family represents a crucial connection in Saudi Arabian history, specifically through the exile period of the Al Saud royal family. When the House of Saud's regional rivals led by Muhammad bin Abdullah Al Rashid conquered Riyadh in 1891, the Al Saud family, including 15-year-old Abdulaziz (future Ibn Saud), initially took refuge with the Al Murrah, a Bedouin tribe in the southern desert of Arabia. This period of protection by the Al Murrah tribe was brief but significant in the survival of the Saudi dynasty. After their stay with the Al Murrah, the Al Saud family moved to Qatar for two months, then to Bahrain briefly, before finally settling in Kuwait where they lived for nearly a decade under Ottoman permission. The Al Murrah's role as protectors during this critical period helped preserve the Saudi lineage that would eventually establish the modern Kingdom of Saudi Arabia when Ibn Saud recaptured Riyadh in 1902.
data.description: A Bedouin tribe from the southern Arabian desert that provided crucial refuge to the exiled Al Saud royal family in 1891, playing a vital role in preserving the Saudi dynasty during their most vulnerable period.
data.originStory: The Al Murrah are described as a Bedouin tribe inhabiting the southern desert of Arabia, though specific details of their origins are not provided in the research.
data.modernStatus: Current status not specified in the research materials.

connections.context: Provided refuge to the exiled Al Saud family in 1891 during their flight from Riyadh

data.history: The Al Ghafiri family belongs to the Ghafiri tribal confederation, one of two major tribal confederations of Oman and the Trucial States (today the United Arab Emirates), the other being the Hinawi. The Ghafiri confederation was established in the mid-8th century and traces its roots to the Nizari or Adnani tribes. The confederation includes multiple tribes such as the Na'im, the Beni Ka'ib, the Beni Kattab and Daramikeh. In the late 19th century, Ghafiri numbering approximately 20,000 people lived in the areas of Buraimi and Su'areb. The confederation played a significant role in political history, with tribal leaders participating in the election of imams who functioned as both temporal and religious leaders. Because of the Ghafiri's support of Saif ibn Sultan II, a major clash occurred between the Ghafiri and Hinawi confederations in 1748 in which leaders of both tribes died. During conflicts and rivalries between imam and sultan, the British eventually played an intermediary role, resulting in a stable Sultanate in Oman lasting between 1920 and 1954.
data.description: The Al Ghafiri are part of the Ghafiri tribal confederation, one of two major tribal groups in Oman and the UAE established in the mid-8th century. They trace their origins to Nizari or Adnani tribes and have played important roles in regional political history.
data.originStory: The Ghafiri confederation traces its roots to the Nizari or Adnani tribes and was established in the mid-8th century as one of two major tribal confederations in the Ibadi Imamate.
data.modernStatus: The Ghafiri confederation continues to exist in modern Oman and UAE, though rivalry with the Hinawi is generally limited to opposing football teams in contemporary times.

data.history: The Al Araimi family appears to be an established Omani family based on the limited research available. Sheikh Dr. Salim Said Al Fannah Al Araimi and Sheikh Mohamed Rashid Al Fannah Al Araimi were co-founders of Galfar Engineering and Contracting in 1972, alongside Indian businessman P. Mohamed Ali in the Sultanate of Oman. This partnership created what would become Oman's largest construction company with EPC capability in Oil & Gas, Roads & Bridges, and Civil Infrastructure sectors. The company has grown to operate with a fleet of more than 8,000 equipment while employing over 20,000 people, making it the largest employer of Omani nationals in the private sector with a turnover above US$1 Billion. Recent academic research from 2025 indicates continued Al Araimi presence in Oman, with Al-Araimi and colleagues publishing research on genetic conditions, suggesting the family maintains involvement in academic and professional spheres within the country.
data.description: An established Omani family known for co-founding Galfar Engineering and Contracting in 1972, which became Oman's largest construction company and private sector employer.
data.modernStatus: The Al Araimi family maintains prominence in Oman through their founding stake in Galfar Engineering and Contracting, the country's largest construction company with over US$1 billion turnover and 20,000+ employees. Family members continue to be involved in academic research and professional activities within Oman.

data.history: The Al Mudahka family's documented presence in Qatar centers around their involvement in media and intellectual pursuits. The family's most prominent modern figure is Faisal Abdulhameed al-Mudahka, who serves as editor-in-chief of Gulf Times, Qatar's first English-language newspaper founded in 1978. The family has also produced Mohammed Ahmed Al-Modiahki (born 1974), Qatar's first chess Grandmaster who achieved the title in 1998 and was awarded the trophy of Player of the Century within Arab Countries. Al-Modiahki's international chess career included participation in nine Chess Olympiads between 1988-2006, winning individual gold medals in 1996 and 1998, and four Arab Chess Championship victories in 1994, 1997, 2000, and 2002. His marriage in 2001 to Chinese Grandmaster Zhu Chen, who subsequently also played for Qatar, represents the family's international connections through intellectual and sporting excellence.
data.description: A Qatari family known for their contributions to media and chess, with members holding prominent positions in journalism and achieving international recognition in competitive chess.

data.history: The Al Bu Shamis is a subsection of the Na'im tribe, one of three major divisions along with the Al Bu Kharaiban and the Khawatir. Unlike the Al Bu Kharaiban who rule Ajman, the Al Bu Shamis have become virtually independent and are closely associated with the Al Bu Falah of Abu Dhabi. They originally held control of the coastal settlement of Ajman until Sheikh Rashid bin Humaid Al Nuaimi and fifty followers took it from them in a short conflict, with the Ajman fort finally falling to Rashid's forces in 1816 or 1817. The Al Bu Shamis maintained significant populations across the Trucial States, with the Darawishah subsection dominating areas like Hamriyah (250 of 300 houses) and Hirah (250 households), while 100 Darawishah houses were found in Sharjah town itself. By the early 20th century, they were involved in various conflicts including the Buraimi Dispute of the 1950s, where some 200 Na'im, Al Bu Shamis and Bani Kaab fought against the British. Despite periodic affirmation of recognition of the Na'im's supreme authority (tamimah), the Al Bu Shamis maintained an almost completely separate identity, with their leader at Al Heera often in strong disagreement or war with the Na'imi Al Bu Kharaiban Ruler of Ajman.
data.description: The Al Bu Shamis is a subsection of the Na'im tribe that has achieved virtual independence while maintaining loose ties to the larger tribal confederation. They are closely associated with Abu Dhabi's Al Bu Falah and historically controlled various settlements across the Trucial States.
data.originStory: The Al Bu Shamis originated as one of three major sections of the Na'im tribal confederation, alongside the Al Bu Kharaiban and the Khawatir. The Na'im tribe as a whole was traditionally centered around the Buraimi oasis and nearby Al Ain, where they expanded at the expense of the Dhawahir tribe.
data.modernStatus: The Al Bu Shamis continue to exist as a distinct tribal group, with many prominent figures in business and politics. The Al Naboodah trading family, descended from Al Bu Shamis members, employs some 16,000 staff and is active across aviation, construction, agriculture and logistics.
data.nameEtymology: Al Shamsi is the singular form of Al Bu Shamis

data.history: Al Gharir (الغرير) is documented as a branch of the historic Uyunid dynasty, which ruled Eastern Arabia from 1076-1253 CE. The family name appears in historical records as 'Gharir b. Shukr b. Ali,' who was instrumental in the assassination of Muhammad b. Ahmad, a prominent Uyunid ruler who had expanded the dynasty's territory from Najd to the Syrian desert and held authority over the pilgrimage route to Mecca from Caliph al-Nasir li-Din Allah. The Uyunids belonged to the Banu Abd al-Qays tribe and established their rule after defeating the Qarmatians with Seljuk Turkish assistance, initially controlling the regions of al-Hasa, Qatif, and Awal (modern Bahrain). The dynasty's internal conflicts and assassinations, including those involving the Gharir branch, ultimately weakened Uyunid power and contributed to their eventual downfall to the Usfurids in 1253 CE. As descendants of this medieval Arab dynasty, Al Gharir represents one of the oldest documented family lineages in the Gulf region, with roots tracing back nearly a millennium to the tribal confederations that shaped early Islamic Eastern Arabia.
data.description: Al Gharir is a historic Gulf family descended from the medieval Uyunid dynasty that ruled Eastern Arabia from 1076-1253 CE. The family name appears in historical records connected to internal dynastic conflicts that contributed to the Uyunids' eventual decline.
data.originStory: The Al Gharir lineage traces back to Gharir b. Shukr b. Ali, a member of the Uyunid ruling family in medieval Eastern Arabia. The Uyunids seized control from the Qarmatians in 1076-1077 CE with Seljuk Turkish military assistance, establishing a dynasty that would rule for 163 years across territories including al-Hasa, Qatif, and the Awal islands (modern Bahrain).

data.history: The Al Mudairis family name appears to be related to the historical Uyunid dynasty through the research on Abdullah bin Ali Al Uyuni, though the specific connection is not explicitly established in the provided materials. The Uyunid dynasty was founded by Abdullah bin Ali Al Uyuni, who ruled from 1074 to 1107 and established the Uyunid Emirate in Eastern Arabia. The dynasty originated from the Banu Abdul Qays tribe, which stemmed from Rabi'a ibn Nizar, described as the ancestor of Eastern Arabian tribes. The family was named Al Uyuni after the village of Al Oyun in the Al-Ahsa Oasis where the tribe once lived. Abdullah bin Ali Al Uyuni successfully revolted against the Qarmatian state, initially starting with only 400 supporters from the Abdul Qays tribe. With eventual Seljuk assistance, he overthrew the Qarmatians and united Eastern Arabia and Bahrain into one state based in Al-Ahsa by 1078.
data.description: A family name potentially connected to the historical Uyunid dynasty that ruled Eastern Arabia from 1074-1107, with possible origins tracing to the Banu Abdul Qays tribe of the Al-Ahsa region.
data.originStory: According to the genealogical records, the lineage traces to the Banu Abdul Qays tribe, though it is unclear from which specific branch. The family was named after the village of Al Oyun in the Al-Ahsa Oasis where the tribe once lived.
data.nameEtymology: The family was named Al Uyuni after the village of Al Oyun in the Al-Ahsa Oasis where the tribe once lived

data.history: The research provides limited information about the Al Rashid Al Khalid family as a distinct genealogical unit. The materials primarily discuss individual figures with the names Rashid or Khalid from different contexts. Sheikh Khalid Mohammad Hamad Al Rashid is documented as a Saudi Arabian citizen arrested in the mid-2000s for his religious expression and associations, with human rights organizations citing his connection to the 'Movement of Reformers' and protests against Danish cartoons of Prophet Muhammad. His detention was extended multiple times, ultimately resulting in a 40-year prison sentence by 2022. The historical Abbasid Caliph Harun al-Rashid (c. 763-809 CE) represents a different lineage entirely, being the fifth Abbasid caliph who established the House of Wisdom in Baghdad and is celebrated in Islamic history and literature. The research also references various Al Khalifa rulers of Bahrain and Saudi royal family members, but does not establish clear genealogical connections between these different Rashid and Khalid lineages to form a unified 'Al Rashid Al Khalid' family structure.
data.description: The research does not provide sufficient evidence for a unified Al Rashid Al Khalid family genealogy, instead documenting separate individuals and lineages bearing these names across different historical periods and geographical locations.
data.nameEtymology: Al-Rashid translates to 'the Just', 'the Upright', or 'the Rightly-Guided' as documented for the historical Abbasid Caliph Harun al-Rashid

data.history: The Al Qubaisat are mentioned in the research as a significant tribal group with historical presence in the UAE region. In 1869, the Qubaisat tribe settled at Khor Al Adaid and attempted to enlist Ottoman support, though this area was claimed by Abu Dhabi with British backing. The British Political Resident Percy Cox confirmed in writing to Abu Dhabi's ruler Zayed bin Khalifa Al Nahyan in 1906 that Khor Al Adaid belonged to his sheikhdom. The Al Qubaisat appear to have maintained important tribal connections, particularly with the Al Nahyan ruling family. Sheikha Salama bint Butti, described as 'an influential lady from the tribe of Al-Qubaisi,' was the mother of several prominent Al Nahyan leaders including Sheikh Sultan bin Zayed (ruler 1922-1926), Sheikh Hazza bin Sultan Al Nahyan, and Sheikh Shakhbut bin Sultan Al Nahyan (ruler 1928-1966). During political upheavals in the 1920s, when Sheikh Khalid (brother of Hazza) was wounded in Abu Dhabi violence, he sought refuge specifically with the Qubaisat, his mother's family, indicating the tribe's continued protective role and influence.
data.description: The Al Qubaisat are a historically significant tribal group in the UAE region, known for their settlement at Khor Al Adaid in 1869 and their important matrimonial connections to the Al Nahyan ruling family of Abu Dhabi.

data.history: The Habus (singular Al Habsi) is an Arab tribe of Ras Al Khaimah in the United Arab Emirates, primarily settled around Khatt, Fahlain and the Hajar Mountain wadis to the east of Ras Al Khaimah city. They are frequently associated with the Hajar Mountain tribes of the Shihuh and Dhahuriyiin, sharing cultural similarities and traditions. The Habus speak the distinctive Shehhi dialect of Arabic, thought to have Himyarite Yemeni origins dating back to the second century BCE. An agrarian people, they cultivated extensive mountainous farmland in the wadis of the Hajar Mountains, particularly settling and farming the mountains of the Rus Al Jibal in Wadi Naqab and Wadi Bih. Their name is said to derive from their original land holdings on Jebel Hibs, a mountain in Ras Al Khaimah. The area around Wadi Naqab was traditionally held by the Naqbiyin tribe, but was gradually bought up by the Habus after the Naqbiyin fell foul of local rulers, likely pre-Qawasim (1695–1740), with many Naqbiyin moving to Dibba and Khor Fakkan. By about 1800, Wadi Naqab was considered Habus territory. Fiercely independent, the Habus successfully resisted taxation attempts by Sheikh Sultan bin Salim Al Qasimi, with an uneasy peace made between Habus Sheikh Hamdan bin Malik and Sultan bin Salim. The tribe continued to be troublesome subjects even into 1963, when they held up a Ras Al Khaimah mineral survey party in protest against Sheikh Saqr and attempted to assert independence.
data.description: An Arab tribe of Ras Al Khaimah, UAE, primarily settled in the Hajar Mountains around Khatt and Fahlain. Known for their agricultural lifestyle, fierce independence, and distinctive Shehhi dialect with Himyarite Yemeni origins.
data.originStory: The Habus name is said to be derived from their original holdings of land on Jebel Hibs, a mountain in Ras Al Khaimah. They speak the Shehhi dialect of Arabic, thought to have Himyarite Yemeni origins dating back to the second century BCE.
data.modernStatus: A Habus tribal festival, established in 2010, showcases the traditions and heritage of the tribe, including handcrafts and perfumery. Many younger members are now returning to build summer houses on their family lands, though many mountain farms and settlements are now abandoned.
data.nameEtymology: Their name is said to be derived from the location of their original holdings of land on the Jebel Hibs, a mountain in Ras Al Khaimah.

data.history: The Al Tabtabaei family has a documented presence in Kuwaiti politics, particularly through Dr. Walid Al-Tabtabaei who emerged as a prominent member of parliament in the late 20th and early 21st centuries. The family name appears consistently in Kuwait's National Assembly records from the 1990s onward, with Walid Al-Tabtabaei serving as an MP who actively participated in grilling government ministers on various issues including information policy, health services, and religious affairs. His political activities spanned multiple parliamentary terms, demonstrating the family's sustained involvement in Kuwait's democratic processes. The research shows Al-Tabtabaei participating in significant political actions including attempts to grill Prime Ministers and various cabinet ministers, indicating his role as an opposition figure willing to challenge government policies. Beyond politics, there appears to be some connection to broader intellectual movements, as referenced in relation to Iranian intellectual circles, though the specific nature of this connection requires further clarification.
data.description: A Kuwaiti family known primarily for their political participation through Dr. Walid Al-Tabtabaei, who served as an active member of parliament involved in government oversight and ministerial questioning.

data.history: The Al Kathir family's most documented historical figure is Ibn Kathir (c. 1300-1373), the renowned Islamic scholar born in Bostra, Syria. Abu al-Fida Isma'il ibn Umar ibn Kathir al-Dimashqi was an Arab Islamic exegete, historian and scholar whose family traced its lineage back to the tribe of Quraysh. He became a leading authority on Sunni Islam, writing the famous Tafsir Ibn Kathir and the fourteen-volume universal history al-Bidaya wa'l-Nihaya. His scholarly influence extended through his students and his works, which gained particular prominence among Salafi and Wahhabi scholars in later centuries. The research also mentions Ibn Kathir al-Makki (665-737 CE), one of the transmitters of the seven canonical Qira'at methods of Quranic recitation, whose family was of Iranian origin and had immigrated to Yemen before settling in Mecca. Additionally, there is reference to the Al Kathiri tribe, one of the largest tribes of Banu Quda'a with populations across Yemen, Oman, Saudi Arabia, Qatar, UAE, and other regions, though the connection between these different Kathir lineages is not established in the research.
data.description: The Al Kathir name appears across multiple historical contexts, most prominently through the medieval Islamic scholar Ibn Kathir (1300-1373) of Syria, whose family claimed Quraysh tribal origins, and various tribal groups including the Yemeni Al Kathiri tribe of Banu Quda'a descent.
data.originStory: According to the research, Ibn Kathir's family traces its lineage back to the tribe of Quraysh, the prominent Arabian tribe. However, the research presents multiple distinct Kathir lineages without establishing clear connections between them.
data.tribalOrigin: Quraysh (for Ibn Kathir's lineage); Banu Quda'a (for Al Kathiri tribe)
data.modernStatus: The research does not provide information about the current business or political status of Al Kathir families in the GCC region.

data.history:
The Al Bu Qitab (also written as Bani Qitab) were a prominent Bedouin Arab tribe in the United Arab Emirates and Oman during the 19th and early 20th centuries. The tribe emerged as a significant force in the interior regions, particularly in areas around Buraimi, Wadi Hatta, and the districts between the mountains and the coast. By the early 1900s, they had become principal rivals to the Bedouin Na'im tribe, gradually gaining predominance over them in certain territories.

The tribe's most notable period of activity occurred in 1905 when they built a fort called Al Hauz at Jabail in Wadi Hatta and began harassing caravans passing through the mountain passes to the Omani Batina coast. This action brought them into conflict with the Na'im tribe of Masfout, who appealed to Sheikh Zayed bin Khalifa Al Nahyan of Abu Dhabi for support. The dispute escalated into a major regional conflict involving multiple Trucial rulers.

The Bani Qitab found strong support from Sheikh Rashid bin Ahmad Al Mualla of Umm Al Quwain, who backed them financially and politically against the coalition led by Zayed bin Khalifa. Despite a meeting of Trucial Sheikhs in Dubai in 1905 that ordered the demolition of their fort, the Bani Qitab's influence continued to grow. Their conflicts with other tribes, particularly the Balush of Dhahirah in 1906, further established their reputation as a formidable fighting force in the interior regions.

By the 1920s, the Bani Qitab remained influential among the interior tribes and were involved in various conflicts, including fighting alongside the Duru and Awamir against the Manasir in Buraimi in 1923. Their strategic position and warrior reputation made them valuable allies to various rulers, and they maintained their independence and influence well into the oil exploration period of the mid-20th century.
data.description: The Al Bu Qitab (Bani Qitab) were a prominent Bedouin Arab tribe known for their warrior traditions and strategic control of mountain passes in the UAE and Oman. They gained particular notoriety in the early 1900s for their conflicts with the Na'im tribe and their alliance with Umm Al Quwain's rulers.
data.modernStatus: Historical records indicate the tribe's influence declined with modernization and oil development, though specific details of their current status are not provided in available sources.

data.history: The research provided focuses on Lebanese Shia figures with the surname Al Naim, specifically leaders of Hezbollah including Naim Qassem, Abbas al-Musawi, and Hashem Safieddine. Naim Qassem was born in February 1953 in Kfar Kila (also referenced as Kfar Fila), Lebanon, into a Shiite family with origins in that town. He grew up in Beirut and became one of the founders of Hezbollah in 1982 following the Iranian Revolution. Qassem served as deputy secretary-general from 1991 to 2024 under both Abbas al-Musawi and Hassan Nasrallah, before becoming the fourth secretary-general of Hezbollah on 29 October 2024 after the assassinations of his predecessors. The research indicates these figures are part of Lebanese Shia clerical and political networks rather than Gulf families, with their activities centered in Lebanon's Beqaa Valley and southern regions. No connection to GCC family genealogy, business history, or tribal lineage is established in the provided research.
data.description: Based on available research, Al Naim appears to refer to Lebanese Shia political and religious figures, particularly Hezbollah leadership, rather than a Gulf family lineage.
data.modernStatus: The research only covers Lebanese political figures with this surname, not Gulf business or tribal entities

data.history: The Al Mazrui family represents two distinct lineages with no verified connection. The first is the Kenyan Al Mazrui family, historically wealthy rulers of Mombasa with deep roots in the Swahili coast. Ali Mazrui (1933-2014) noted his family 'had previously been the rulers of Mombasa' and his father Al-Amin Bin Ali Mazrui served as Chief Islamic Judge in Kenya Colony's Kadhi courts. This branch traces to the Azd tribes through al-'Atik b. al-Asad b. Imran, who settled in Dibba, with the Azd originally from Yemen migrating north after the Marib Dam's destruction. The second lineage is the UAE Al Mazrui family, prominently represented by Shamma bint Suhail bin Faris Al Mazrui, current UAE Minister of Community Development. Her father Suhail bin Faris Ghanim Ateish Al Mazrui is a businessman who chaired Dubai Investments board for over 20 years, with her brothers holding senior positions in business and sovereign wealth funds. Born in Abu Dhabi in 1993, Shamma became the youngest UAE cabinet member at age 22 when appointed Minister of State for Youth Affairs in 2016.
data.description: Al Mazrui comprises two distinct family lineages: a Kenyan branch with historical ruling status in Mombasa and scholarly prominence, and a UAE branch with significant contemporary political and business influence.
data.originStory: The Kenyan Al Mazrui trace their ancestry to the Azd tribes through al-'Atik b. al-Asad b. Imran, who settled in Dibba. The Azd originally hailed from Yemen and migrated north after the destruction of the Marib Dam.
data.modernStatus: UAE branch: Prominent in government and business with Shamma Al Mazrui serving as Minister of Community Development, family involved in Dubai Investments and Mubadala. Kenyan branch: Historical scholarly prominence through Ali Mazrui's academic legacy.
