data.history: Based on the research provided, there is clear documentation of Mir Chakar Khan Rind (1468-1565), a prominent Baloch chieftain who founded the Second Baloch Confederacy from 1487-1512. According to Baloch history, Chakar Rind descended from Rind Khan, a son of Jalal Khan, from whom many Baloch tribes trace their lineage. Mir Chakar became head of the Rind tribe at age 18 after his father Mir Shehaq Rind's death, though his birthplace remains debated among Baloch historians, with Kolwah Balochistan being one suggested location. His leadership was marked by the great civil war against the Lashari tribe, known as the 'Thirty Years' War,' which resulted in thousands of deaths and became a significant part of Baloch history. After leaving Balochistan due to ongoing conflicts, Mir Chakar moved through various territories including the Sanghar Desert in Taunsa, Dera Ghazi Khan, where he established settlements. He eventually allied with the Multan king and his forces helped conquer Delhi, with the Delhi king reportedly praising the unity and bravery of the Baloch warriors. Mir Chakar later settled in Satghara, Okara, where his son Mir Shahdad Khan's forces joined Emperor Humayun's Mughal army in 1555, leading to Mir Chakar receiving a vast Jagir from the emperor. He ruled this territory until his death in 1565, and his descendants moved to different parts of the region and Middle East, often adding 'Rind' as a suffix to their names.
data.description: The Al Rind family traces its lineage to the historic Baloch Rind tribe, specifically to Mir Chakar Khan Rind (1468-1565), a prominent chieftain who founded the Second Baloch Confederacy and later allied with the Mughal Empire. His descendants spread across various regions, maintaining the Rind name as part of their identity.
data.originStory: According to Baloch history, the Rind lineage descends from Rind Khan, who was a son of Jalal Khan, from whom numerous Baloch tribes trace their ancestry. Mir Chakar Khan Rind, born in 1468, became the most prominent figure of this lineage, leading the tribe through significant conflicts and territorial expansions before establishing lasting alliances with Mughal rulers.
data.modernStatus: Contemporary members of the Al Rind family maintain their historical connection to the Baloch Rind tribe, with two major educational institutions named after Mir Chakar Khan Rind: Mir Chakar Khan Rind University in Luni, Sibi (Balochistan) and Mir Chakar Khan Rind University of Technology in Dera Ghazi Khan (Punjab).

data.history: Based on the available research, the Al Leghari family is documented primarily through Pakistani political and entertainment figures. Aurangzeb Leghari, born in 1944 in Rahimabad, Rahim Yar Khan (then British India), represents the entertainment branch as a prominent TV actor since the 1970s. The family gained significant political prominence through Awais Leghari (born 1971), who serves as a Pakistani politician, and notably through Farooq Leghari, who served as President of Pakistan and played a crucial role in dismissing Prime Minister Benazir Bhutto's government in 1996. The research indicates the family has roots in the Punjab region of Pakistan, with documented presence in Rahim Yar Khan district. The name appears in various political contexts throughout Pakistan's modern history, suggesting the family has maintained influence in both cultural and political spheres across multiple generations.
data.description: Al Leghari is a Pakistani family with documented presence in entertainment and politics, notably including TV actor Aurangzeb Leghari and President Farooq Leghari who dismissed Benazir Bhutto's government in 1996.

data.history: The Al Suhail family's most documented connection to Gulf royalty is through Sheikha Shamsa bint Suhail Al Mazrouei, who married Sheikh Khalifa bin Zayed Al Nahyan, the second President of the UAE. According to Wikipedia sources, she is from the Al Khamis family of the Al Shikr branch of the Mazaria tribe in Liwa. The marriage took place on October 18, 1964, making her the mother of eight children with Sheikh Khalifa, including Sultan bin Khalifa Al Nahyan and Mohammed bin Khalifa Al Nahyan. This union established significant political connections within the UAE's ruling structure. The family name 'Suhail' appears to reference the Arabic star name meaning 'easy' or 'gentle,' which has traditional astronomical significance in Arab culture, particularly referring to the star Canopus and other southern hemisphere stars visible from the Arabian Peninsula.

data.history: The House of Fayez (Al-Fayez) is a noble sheikhly Jordanian family that heads the major Jordanian clan Bani Sakher. The Al-Fayez are the ruling branch of the Twaqa, descendants of the 14th century AD Twayq bin Ahmad Al-Djarah, who was a Bani Sakher Sheikh. The Bani Sakher are descendants of the 3rd century Sakher, a great grandson of Julhamah bin Udad, the 2nd AD progenitor of the Banu Tayy Tribe which originated from Kahlan, the son of King Sheba, who was the great-grandson of Qahtan in Ancient Yemen. The family's influence and prominence peaked under Fendi Al-Fayez in the 1840s, who gradually became the leader of the entire Bani Sakher and ruled large parts of Jordan and Palestine, including the ancient Kingdoms of Moab and Ammon, and parts of modern-day Saudi Arabia until the late 1860s when battles with the Ottoman Empire decreased their resources. After Fendi's death in 1879, his son Sattam led the tribe toward a more sedentary lifestyle, followed by Mithqal Alfayez who established them as a permanent political power in modern Jordan. Mithqal was one of the first shaykhs to meet Abdullah bin Al-Hussein in 1921 and invited him to establish the Transjordanian emirate in Amman, making it Jordan's capital. The family was the largest owner of land in Jordan and owned portions of modern day Palestine, with Mithqal being the single largest owner of private land in the kingdom in 1922.
data.description: A noble sheikhly Jordanian family that heads the Bani Sakher clan, originating from the Banu Tayy tribe of Ancient Yemen. They played a central role in founding modern Jordan and establishing Amman as its capital.
data.originStory: The origin of the name is from the progenitor of the house, Fayez bin Fadel Al-Tayy. Fayez is derived from the Arabic word Fa'iz, meaning 'Victorious' in Arabic. Fayez's full name is Fayez bin Fadel bin Rahma bin Ghbayen bin Im'jayd bin Twayq bin Hamad Bin Al-Djarah Bin Al-Ahmadi Bin Al-Fahaad Al-Sukhari. The Bani Sakher were well established in the Arabian Peninsula and in Egypt since at least the 6th century AD, however they gained international prominence in the 14th century AD with the capture and killing of Sultan Sha'ban II in 1377 in Aqaba.
data.modernStatus: Currently headed by former Prime Minister Faisal Al-Fayez, who is the only person in Jordanian history to have been Prime Minister, President of the Senate, Speaker of the House of Representatives, and Head of the Royal Hashemite Court. The family remains active in Jordanian and Arabian politics with multiple members holding ministerial and ambassadorial positions.
data.nameEtymology: Fayez is derived from the Arabic word Fa'iz, meaning 'Victorious' in Arabic

data.history: The Al Nafjan family appears to be connected to Saudi women's rights activism through Eman al-Nafjan, a prominent Saudi blogger and activist who has been involved in campaigns against the driving ban and male guardianship system. According to the research, Eman al-Nafjan was detained in May 2018 alongside other prominent women's rights activists including Loujain al-Hathloul, Aisha al-Mana, Aziza al-Yousef, and Madeha al-Ajroush as part of a crackdown on feminists. She was later released on bail in March 2019 along with Aziza al-Yousef and Dr. Rokaya Mohareb. The family name appears in the context of the Women to Drive Movement and anti-male guardianship campaigns that gained prominence during the 2010s in Saudi Arabia. Al-Nafjan, along with other activists, received the 2019 PEN America/Barbey Freedom to Write Award for her women's rights activism.
data.description: The Al Nafjan family is associated with Saudi women's rights activism through Eman al-Nafjan, a blogger and activist involved in campaigns against driving bans and the male guardianship system.

data.history: The Al Shubaili family appears in the research primarily through Ahmed Mohammed Al-Shubaili, a member of the 115th Infantry Brigade who was severely wounded in an attack in Lawdar, Abyan Governorate, Yemen in August 2017. His son Al-Khader Ahmed Mohammed Al-Shubaili was killed in the same attack by suspected AQAP gunmen. The family name also appears in a list of Amnesty International-designated prisoners of conscience with Abdulaziz al-Shubaili. Additionally, the research mentions Abdul Rahman Al-Shubaili as a journalist who wrote about poet Taher Zamakhshari. The research also references Mohamed Al-Hamad Al-Shubaili, who received the Knight Grand Companion (S.I.M.P.) honor from the Order of the Crown of Pahang in Malaysia in 1985, receiving the title Dato' Indera. data.history: The Al Qadi family is documented in Jordan, where they founded the Arab Jordan Investment Bank in Amman. Wael Abdulkader Al-Qadi, born November 25, 1969 in Jordan, represents the contemporary generation of this family. He was educated at Westminster School in London and later earned a degree in Computer Science with Business Administration at Boston University in 1991. In February 2016, Wael and his family purchased Bristol Rovers football club in England, marking their expansion into international business ventures. Following his father Abdulkadir's death in June 2020, Wael consolidated control of the club by purchasing family members' shares, retaining only 10% with his brother Samer. The family's business interests span banking and sports ownership, demonstrating their evolution from Middle Eastern banking into diversified international investments.

data.history: The Bin Talal family represents multiple distinct lineages across the Arabian Peninsula and Jordan, with no single unified origin. The most prominent branch is the Saudi royal line descended from Prince Talal bin Abdulaziz Al Saud (1931-2018), son of King Abdulaziz and his Armenian wife Munaiyir. Munaiyir's family had escaped from the Ottoman Empire between 1915-1923 during the Armenian upheavals, and she was presented to King Abdulaziz in 1921 when she was 12 years old. Their son Talal became a controversial figure known as 'The Red Prince' for his liberal political views and leadership of the Free Princes Movement in the 1960s, advocating for constitutional monarchy and democratic reforms. The Jordanian Hashemite branch includes King Talal of Jordan (1909-1972) and his descendants Prince Muhammad bin Talal and Prince Hassan bin Talal, who served as Crown Prince of Jordan from 1965-1999. Prince Hassan married Sarvath Ikramullah, whose family belonged to the feudal aristocracy of Bhopal state in central India. The Saudi Bin Talal line produced notable business figures like Prince Al Waleed bin Talal, who became one of the world's wealthiest individuals through Kingdom Holding Company, though he was detained during Crown Prince Mohammed bin Salman's 2017 anti-corruption campaign and released after a reported $6 billion settlement.
data.description: The Bin Talal represents multiple royal lineages, including Saudi princes descended from King Abdulaziz and an Armenian wife, and Jordanian Hashemite royals. Notable for producing both political reformers and international business magnates.
data.originStory: The Saudi branch originates from King Abdulaziz's marriage to Munaiyir, an Armenian woman whose family escaped Ottoman persecution between 1915-1923. The Jordanian branch descends from the Hashemite dynasty, tracing lineage to the Prophet Muhammad through Hassan ibn Ali.
data.modernStatus: The Saudi branch includes Prince Al Waleed bin Talal, billionaire owner of Kingdom Holding Company with major stakes in international hotels, media, and technology companies. The Jordanian branch remains active in international diplomacy and humanitarian work, with Prince Hassan leading various UN and international organizations.

data.history: The Mahfouz family represents multiple distinct lineages across the Arab world, with no confirmed connection between branches. The most documented branch originates from Egypt, exemplified by Nobel Prize-winning author Naguib Mahfouz (1911-2006), whose full name was Naguib Mahfouz Abdelaziz Ibrahim Ahmed Al-Basha. His family were devout Muslims from Old Cairo's Gamaleya quarter, with his father Abdel-Aziz Ibrahim working as a civil servant and his mother Fatimah being the daughter of Al-Azhar sheikh Mustafa Qasheesha. A separate Saudi branch emerged through Salem bin Mahfouz, who rose from being a small-time money-changer to founding the National Commercial Bank (NCB), Saudi Arabia's first private bank. His son Khalid bin Mahfouz (1949-2009) became a billionaire banker and businessman, serving as NCB chairman and ranking among the world's wealthiest individuals with an estimated $6 billion fortune. A third branch is represented by Mahfouz Marei Mubarak bin Mahfouz (born 1969), a Saudi businessman who became prominent through charitable donations to British royal causes, receiving a CBE in 2016.
data.description: The Mahfouz surname appears across multiple unconnected Arab families, most notably the Egyptian literary family of Nobel laureate Naguib Mahfouz and the Saudi banking dynasty founded by Salem bin Mahfouz. These represent distinct lineages with no established genealogical connection.
data.modernStatus: The Saudi Mahfouz branch remains prominent in banking through the bin Mahfouz family's continued involvement with NCB, though government intervention occurred in 1999. The Egyptian branch gained international recognition through literature. The contemporary Saudi branch maintains charitable and business activities with international connections.
data.nameEtymology: The name 'Mahfouz' derives from Arabic محفوظ meaning 'protected' or 'preserved'. Egyptian author Naguib Mahfouz was named after the obstetrician Naguib Pasha Mahfouz who delivered him during a difficult birth.

data.history: The Batarfi family appears to have origins connected to Yemen, as evidenced by Ayman Saeed Abdullah Batarfi, a Yemeni orthopedic surgeon who graduated from medical school in Pakistan and pursued postdoctoral studies there. Ayman Batarfi was involved with the al Wafa Humanitarian Organization, which was headquartered in Saudi Arabia and had connections to Afghanistan, where he purchased medical supplies and equipment. He was captured and held at Guantanamo Bay detention center from 2004 to 2009, during which time he was accused of having associations with al-Qaeda operations in Afghanistan, particularly during the 2001 Battle of Tora Bora. Another notable family member is Khalid Batarfi, mentioned in connection with AQAP (Al-Qaeda in the Arabian Peninsula) leadership, though the exact family relationship is not specified in the research. The family name also appears in Saudi contexts, with Khaled M. Batarfi writing op-eds in Saudi Gazette, suggesting the family may have connections across the Arabian Peninsula.

data.history: The Bakhashab family emerged as prominent businessmen and educational pioneers in Saudi Arabia during the 20th century. Muhammad Abu Bakr Bakhashab was a key figure in establishing King Abdulaziz University in Jeddah, serving on the preparatory committee in 1964 and helping to found what became the largest university in Saudi Arabia. The family also established the Bakhashab Transport Company in 1948, which played a crucial role in transporting Hajj pilgrims at various holy sites, effectively ending the use of camels for pilgrimage transportation by 1950. The company operated alongside the Arabian Transport Company and proved highly effective in modernizing pilgrimage logistics during the post-World War II era.
data.description: A Saudi Arabian business family known for their contributions to education through founding King Abdulaziz University and transportation through the Bakhashab Transport Company, which modernized Hajj pilgrimage logistics.
data.modernStatus: The family maintains prominence through their historical contributions to Saudi education and transportation infrastructure, with their transport company having been instrumental in modernizing Hajj pilgrimage services.

data.history: The Al Faraj family name derives from multiple historical origins in the Islamic world, with notable figures bearing this name spanning several centuries. The most prominent historical figure was Abu al-Faraj Abd al-Rahman ibn Ali Al-Jawzi (c. 1116-1201), a renowned Hanbali scholar from Baghdad whose ninth-generation forefather, Jafar ibn Abdullah Al-Bakri, was known as al-Jawzi, hence establishing one line of the family name. Ibn al-Jawzi came from a wealthy family involved in the copper trade and descended from Abu Bakr As-Siddiq through a documented lineage. Another significant Al Faraj figure was Abu al-Faraj Ali ibn al-Husayn al-Isfahani (897-967 CE), a distinguished writer, historian, and musicologist based in Baghdad, who was of Arab-Quraysh origin and descended from the Umayyad caliph Marwan II. In the Nasrid period of Granada, the name appears again with Abu Said Faraj ibn Ismail (born 1248), who married Fatima bint al-Ahmar and served as governor of Málaga under Muhammad II. These historical records show the Al Faraj name associated with scholarly, administrative, and political roles across different regions and time periods, though no direct genealogical connection between these various Al Faraj lines is established in the available sources.
data.description: Al Faraj is a family name with multiple historical origins, most notably associated with prominent Islamic scholars, historians, and administrators across Baghdad, Isfahan, and Al-Andalus from the 9th to 13th centuries.
data.nameEtymology: The name Al Faraj derives from 'faraj' meaning relief or deliverance in Arabic. In the case of Ibn al-Jawzi's lineage, it traces to his ninth-generation forefather Jafar ibn Abdullah Al-Bakri who was known as al-Jawzi, while Abu al-Faraj al-Isfahani's name follows the common Arabic naming pattern of 'Abu al-Faraj' (father of relief).

data.history: The Al Shami family name appears to be associated with Syria ("al-Shami" meaning "of/from Syria"), as evidenced by several notable individuals with this surname. Leila al-Shami is a British-Syrian human rights defender whose father was a communist and former political prisoner exiled from Ba'athist Syria, indicating a Syrian political background spanning generations. Her family maintained connections to Syria through the Damascus Spring period of 2000-2001, when she worked with Syrian opposition figures. Ali Al Shami represents a separate Lebanese branch of the family, being a Shia Lebanese academic born in 1945 who served as Lebanon's minister of foreign affairs from 2009-2011. The research also references Ahmed Muhammad al-Shami as a royalist foreign minister during the North Yemen Civil War, suggesting additional branches in Yemen. However, the research provides limited information about the family's broader genealogy, tribal origins, or comprehensive historical development across the Gulf region.
data.description: The Al Shami surname appears across multiple Arab countries, with documented branches in Syria, Lebanon, and Yemen, though specific tribal origins and comprehensive genealogy remain unclear from available research.

data.history: Based on the research provided, Al Adnani refers to the genealogical classification of Arabs descended from Adnan, who according to Islamic tradition was a direct descendant of Prophet Ismail (Ishmael). The Adnani Arabs are distinguished from Qahtani Arabs and represent the northern, central and western Arabian tribal groups. According to Islamic prophetic tradition, the genealogy from Adnan to Prophet Muhammad comprises 21 generations, with the lineage documented through various tribal chiefs who ruled in the Hejaz region. The research indicates that while Islamic tradition maintains this lineage, the connection from Adnan to Ismail is considered lost by most scholars, and many aspects of the genealogical claims are viewed as speculative rather than historically verified.
data.description: Al Adnani represents a broad genealogical classification of northern Arabian tribes claiming descent from Adnan, who is traditionally considered a descendant of Prophet Ismail in Islamic genealogy.
data.originStory: According to Islamic tradition, the Adnani Arabs descend from Adnan, who was a direct descendant of Prophet Ismail (Ishmael). Islamic tradition states that Adnan was the ancestor of the northern, central and western Arabian tribes, though scholars note that the exact genealogical connections are largely speculative.
data.nameEtymology: Al Adnani derives from Adnan, the legendary ancestor of northern Arabian tribes in Islamic genealogical tradition

data.history: The Al Andalusi family name derives from al-Andalus, the Arabic name for the Muslim-ruled territories of the Iberian Peninsula from 711 to 1492. The term 'Andalusi' literally means 'of al-Andalus' and was used to identify people, culture, language, and traditions originating from this region. After the fall of Granada in 1492 and subsequent expulsions of Muslims (Moriscos) between 1609-1614, many Andalusi families fled to North Africa, particularly Morocco, Tunisia, Algeria, and Egypt. The Andalusi communities significantly influenced local dialects and preserved their cultural traditions, especially through Andalusi classical music. From North Africa, some Andalusi descendants may have migrated to other parts of the Islamic world, including the Gulf region, though the specific migration path of any particular Al Andalusi family to the GCC is not documented in the available research. The name represents a geographic and cultural identity rather than a specific tribal lineage, connecting bearers to the rich intellectual and cultural legacy of Muslim Iberia.
data.description: Al Andalusi is a geographic surname indicating ancestry from al-Andalus (Muslim Iberia, 711-1492), with descendants scattered across North Africa and potentially other Islamic regions after the Reconquista and Morisco expulsions.
data.originStory: The family name originates from al-Andalus, the Muslim territories of the Iberian Peninsula conquered in 711 CE by Tariq ibn Ziyad and Musa ibn Nusayr. After the fall of Granada in 1492 and mass expulsions of Moriscos in the early 17th century, Andalusi refugees fled primarily to North Africa where they preserved their cultural identity and influenced local communities.
data.nameEtymology: Al-Andalusi means 'of al-Andalus,' derived from the Arabic name for Muslim-ruled Iberian Peninsula. The etymology of 'al-Andalus' itself is disputed, traditionally linked to the Vandals, though alternative theories suggest connections to Atlantis, Gothic terms, or pre-Roman substrates.

migrationPath.description: Mass exodus of Andalusi Muslims following the fall of Granada and subsequent Morisco expulsions, with refugees settling primarily in Morocco, Tunisia, Algeria, and Egypt

timelineEvents.description: Tariq ibn Ziyad leads conquest establishing al-Andalus, origin of Andalusi identity

timelineEvents.description: Last Muslim stronghold surrenders to Catholic Monarchs, beginning of Andalusi diaspora

timelineEvents.description: Mass expulsion of remaining Muslims from Spain, forcing final Andalusi migration

data.history: The Al Uzbeki family appears to be connected to the Central Asian Arabic communities that migrated to various regions following the early Islamic conquests. Based on the research on Central Asian Arabic varieties and Uzbek history, Arab communities first moved to Central Asia in the 8th century during the Muslim conquests, with about 50,000 Arabic families reportedly settled in Iranian Khorasan, northern Afghanistan, and southern Turkmenistan. These Arabic-speaking communities inhabited areas in Samarqand, Bukhara, Qashqadarya, Surkhandarya (present-day Uzbekistan), and Khatlon (present-day Tajikistan), as well as Afghanistan. Most Central Asian Arabs lived in isolated communities and did not favor intermarriages with the local population, which helped their Arabic language survive in a multilingual environment until the 20th century. By the 1880s, many Arab pastoralists migrated to northern Afghanistan from what is now Uzbekistan and Tajikistan following the Russian conquest of Central Asia, though these Arabs eventually adopted Dari and Uzbek languages. The establishment of Soviet rule in Uzbekistan and Tajikistan caused major linguistic and identity changes for Arab communities, as they were forced to abandon nomadic lifestyles and gradually mixed with Uzbeks, Tajiks, and Turkmen.
data.description: The Al Uzbeki family appears to trace connections to the Central Asian Arabic communities that settled in the region during the 8th century Islamic conquests, maintaining Arabic linguistic and cultural traditions in Central Asia.
data.originStory: Arab communities first migrated to Central Asia in the 8th century during the Muslim conquests and were later joined by groups of Arabs from Balkh and Andkhoy in present-day Afghanistan. These communities settled in areas including Samarqand, Bukhara, and other regions of what is now Uzbekistan and Tajikistan.
data.modernStatus: Current status unknown from provided research
data.nameEtymology: The name 'Uzbeki' likely derives from the Turkic ethnic group name 'Uzbek,' which has disputed origins including being eponymously named after Oghuz Khagan (Oghuz Beg), meaning 'independent/genuine man' from Öz (self) and bek/bey/beg, or from Uğuz-bek meaning 'leader of an oğuz.'

migrationPath.description: Initial Arab migration during Islamic conquests, settlement of Arabic families in Iranian Khorasan and Central Asian cities
Migration of Arab pastoralists to northern Afghanistan following Russian conquest of Central Asia

timelineEvents.title: Migration to Afghanistan
timelineEvents.description: Arab pastoralists migrate from Uzbekistan and Tajikistan to northern Afghanistan due to Russian conquest

data.history:
The Al Zinjibari family name appears to be derived from Zinjibar, the provincial capital of Abyan Governorate in Yemen. The most documented member is Jalal Mohsen Saleh Baleedi al-Marqashi (1981-2016), who adopted the nom de guerre 'Abu Hamza al-Zinjibari' due to his residency in Zinjibar. Born in al-Sawad village in al Wade'a district, Abyan, his father was a brigadier general in the Yemeni military. Baleedi belonged to the al-Marqisha tribe, described as one of the largest tribes in Abyan.

Baleedi worked as an imam at a mosque in Zinjibar until 2011, when he was appointed emir of the newly-established Islamist militant group Ansar al-Sharia by al-Qaeda in the Arabian Peninsula (AQAP). His tribal membership and residency in Zinjibar were cited as factors in AQAP granting him this position to appeal to locals. He led the group's capture of several towns in southern Yemen during 2011-2012, including Zinjibar itself in May 2011.

After government offensives forced his retreat from Abyan in 2012, Baleedi relocated first to al-Mahfad district and later to Hadhramaut, where he became AQAP's provincial commander. He was killed by a US drone strike on February 3, 2016, while traveling through the Marqasha tribal area of Abyan. Following his death, his brother Tawfiq Baleedi was reportedly appointed by AQAP as the new emir of Zinjibar, though this led to internal power struggles within the organization.
data.description: A Yemeni family name associated with Zinjibar in Abyan Governorate, most notably represented by militant leader Jalal Baleedi who adopted 'al-Zinjibari' as his nom de guerre. Connected to the al-Marqisha tribe of Abyan.
data.originStory: The Al Zinjibari name derives from the city of Zinjibar, provincial capital of Abyan Governorate in Yemen, where family members resided and operated.
data.modernStatus: Family associated with Abyan region in Yemen; some members involved in militant activities in early 21st century
data.nameEtymology: Derived from Zinjibar, the provincial capital of Abyan Governorate in Yemen

data.history: The Al Samman family appears to have multiple distinct branches across different regions and time periods, with no clear connection established between them in the available research. The most historically documented branch traces to Muhammad bin Abdul Karim as-Samman al-Madani (1718-1775), a Sunni Islamic scholar born in Medina who was descended from the Islamic prophet Muhammad through the line of Hasan bin Ali. His family came from the tribe of Quraish, and he became a prominent Faqih, hadith expert, and historian who served as caretaker of Medina and guardian of Muhammad's tomb. He initially studied in the Khalwatiyya Sufi order in Damascus under Sheikh Mustafa Bakri before developing his own approach known as the Sammaniyya Tariqa, which spread to Indonesia through his students. A separate Syrian branch is represented by the prominent Damascene family of Ghadah Al-Samman (born 1942), a renowned writer and novelist whose father Ahmed Al-Samman served as president of the University of Damascus. This branch appears to be an established conservative Damascene family with no indicated connection to the Medinan scholarly lineage. The research provides no evidence linking these different Al Samman branches or establishing any unified family origin or migration pattern.
data.description: Al Samman represents multiple distinct family branches, including a historical Medinan scholarly lineage descended from Quraish and tracing to Prophet Muhammad, and a separate prominent Damascene family known for academic and literary contributions.
data.originStory: The Medinan branch descends from the Islamic prophet Muhammad through Hasan bin Ali, son of Fatimah az-Zahra, with the family coming from the tribe of Quraish. The Syrian branch's origins are not specified in the research.
data.modernStatus: The Syrian branch includes notable literary and academic figures, with Ahmed Al-Samman having served as president of the University of Damascus and his daughter Ghadah Al-Samman being a prominent contemporary Arab writer and novelist.

data.history: The Al Naboodah family appears in historical records as an established Emirati merchant family, with Saeed & Mohammed Al Naboodah Group representing one of the UAE's prominent family business conglomerates operating across multiple industries. The family's business operations have evolved from traditional trading activities to become a diversified group with significant holdings. Abdulla Saeed Juma Al Naboodah, a third-generation family member, has emerged as a prominent businessman and sports administrator, founding Phoenix Capital private investment firm in 2007 and establishing Dubai Basketball in 2024. The family has maintained significant involvement in UAE business and sports administration, with Abdulla serving on boards of major institutions including Commercial Bank of Dubai, UAE Football Association, and the European Tour Group. Saeed Juma Al-Naboodah participated in the constitutional development of the UAE, being named among the 28 members of the constituent committee established in 1975 to prepare the draft permanent constitution for the newly formed state.
data.description: The Al Naboodah family is an established Emirati merchant family operating the Saeed & Mohammed Al Naboodah Group, a major UAE-based conglomerate. The family has produced prominent business leaders and played roles in UAE's constitutional development and sports administration.
data.modernStatus: The family operates the Al Naboodah Group, a UAE-based family conglomerate with operations across multiple industries. Current generation includes Abdulla Saeed Juma Al Naboodah who serves as shareholder in the group, founder of Phoenix Capital investment firm, and founder/chairman of Dubai Basketball. The family maintains board positions across major UAE institutions including Commercial Bank of Dubai and UAE Football Association.

data.history: The Al Bu Falasah (also known as Al-Falasi) is a section of the Bani Yas tribal federation that has been the dominant power throughout most of what is now the United Arab Emirates. In 1833, a large group of the Al Bu Falasah section of the Bani Yas seceded to Dubai during the pearling season, under the leadership of Maktoum bin Butti bin Sohal and Obeid bin Said bin Rashid, escaping the violence of Sheikh Khalifah of Abu Dhabi following a coup that removed Sheikh Tahnun as Ruler of Abu Dhabi. The migration involved some 800 members who disagreed with the actions of the new Ruler, Sheikh Khalifa bin Shakhbut Al Nahyan, and moved north to Dubai, which at the time consisted of a settlement of some 250 houses at Shindagha and the Al Fahidi Fort. The following autumn, the bulk of their relatives joined them and virtually all of the Al Bu Falasah were domiciled in Dubai from then on. After Obeid bin Said died of old age in 1836, Maktoum bin Butti took the reins as sole ruler and established the Al Maktoum dynasty that rules Dubai today. The Al Maktoum family, as the ruling branch of the Al Bu Falasah, has controlled Dubai since 1833, transforming it from a pearling and fishing village into a major regional trading hub and eventually into the modern cosmopolitan city it is today.
data.description: The Al Bu Falasah is a section of the Bani Yas tribal federation that seceded from Abu Dhabi in 1833 to establish Dubai. Their ruling branch, the Al Maktoum family, has governed Dubai continuously since then.
data.originStory: The Al Bu Falasah were originally part of the Bani Yas tribal federation based in Abu Dhabi. In 1833, they seceded during the pearling season under joint leadership of Maktoum bin Butti bin Sohal and Obeid bin Said bin Rashid, fleeing the violence that followed a coup against Sheikh Tahnun bin Shakhbut Al Nahyan.
data.modernStatus: The Al Maktoum branch of Al Bu Falasah rules Dubai as an absolute monarchy. Sheikh Mohammed bin Rashid Al Maktoum currently serves as Ruler of Dubai, Vice President and Prime Minister of the UAE. The family owns vast assets including Godolphin horse racing stables and controls Dubai's government and major economic initiatives.
data.nameEtymology: Al Bu Falasah is now also known as Al-Falasi. The House of Al Falasi coat of arms is described as إمارة دبي (Emirate of Dubai) represented by a Falcon, the national bird of the UAE.

migrationPath.description: Large-scale migration of approximately 800 Al Bu Falasah members led by Maktoum bin Butti and Obeid bin Said, escaping violence following political upheaval in Abu Dhabi

data.history: The House of Maktoum (Al Maktoum) is the ruling royal family of the Emirate of Dubai, established in 1833 when about 800 members of the Bani Yas tribe, under joint leadership of Sheikh Maktoum bin Butti and Obeid bin Said, took over Dubai. After Obeid bin Said died in 1836, Maktoum bin Butti became sole ruler and established the Al Maktoum dynasty. The family is a branch of the Bani Yas clan, sharing lineage with the Al Nahyan dynasty of Abu Dhabi. The Al Maktoum dynasty has ruled Dubai continuously since 1833, transforming it from a small settlement around Dubai Creek into a modern port city and regional commercial hub. Within the UAE federation established in 1971, the Al Maktoum family holds the hereditary positions of Prime Minister, Vice President and Minister of Defence, while the Al Nahyan clan holds the presidency.
data.description: The ruling royal family of Dubai since 1833, descended from the Bani Yas tribe, known for transforming Dubai into a major commercial and aviation hub in the Gulf region.
data.originStory: In 1833, approximately 800 members of the Bani Yas tribe under joint leadership of Sheikh Maktoum bin Butti and Obeid bin Said took control of Dubai. Following Obeid bin Said's death in 1836, Maktoum bin Butti established the Al Maktoum dynasty as sole ruler.
data.modernStatus: Currently rules Dubai under Sheikh Mohammed bin Rashid Al Maktoum (since 2006). Holds UAE Vice Presidency, Prime Ministership, and Defence Ministry. Controls major assets including Emirates airline, Godolphin horse racing stable, Dubai International Airport, and Al Maktoum International Airport.

data.history: The 'Emirates NBD families' represents a network of influential Emirati business families connected to Emirates NBD, one of the UAE's largest banking groups formed in 2007. The research reveals key figures like Sheikh Ahmed bin Saeed Al Maktoum, who serves as chairman of Emirates NBD and is a member of Dubai's ruling Al Maktoum family. Born in 1958, Ahmed is the youngest son of Dubai's former ruler Sheikh Saeed bin Maktoum bin Hasher Al Maktoum and half-brother of former ruler Sheikh Rashid bin Saeed Al Maktoum. His career began in 1985 when he was appointed president of the Dubai Department of Civil Aviation and chairman of Emirates airline. The banking sector has been central to UAE's economic diversification efforts, with Emirates NBD playing a crucial role in financing major developments. The bank has been involved in significant real estate transactions, including deals with companies like Binghatti Properties, where Emirates Real Estate Fund managed by Emirates NBD acquired properties worth hundreds of millions of dirhams. These families represent the intersection of traditional ruling house authority and modern financial institutional power in the UAE's rapid economic transformation.
data.description: A network of influential Emirati families connected to Emirates NBD bank, including members of Dubai's ruling Al Maktoum family, who have played central roles in the UAE's banking sector and economic diversification since the bank's formation in 2007.
data.modernStatus: Key stakeholders and leadership of Emirates NBD, one of the UAE's largest banks, with significant influence in real estate financing, aviation industry investments, and economic policy implementation across the emirates

data.history: The Al Qasimi are a Hashemite dynasty and tribe that currently rules Sharjah and Ras Al Khaimah, forming two of the seven emirates of the United Arab Emirates. According to the research, they are one of the longest reigning royal families in the Arabian peninsula, with their ancestors being Huwala who migrated between the Arabian Peninsula and Persia, maintaining rulership over the town of Lengeh on the coast of Hormozgan province until its annexation by Iran in 1887. The Qawasim tribe itself is of Hashemite origin, descending from Husayn ibn Ali, through their eponymous ancestor Al Qasim bin Idris bin Ja'far al-Zaki. By the early 19th century, the Qawasim emerged as a maritime power based both in Ras Al Khaimah on the southern shore of the Persian Gulf and Qishm, Bandar Abbas and Lingeh on the northern shore. During the 18th century, they embraced the Muwahhidun (Wahhabi) movement and became a close ally of the Emirate of Diriyah. Their maritime activities and control of Gulf trade routes led to conflicts with the British East India Company, who branded them as 'pirates,' resulting in major British military campaigns against them in 1809 and 1819. Following the General Maritime Treaty of 1820, the Al Qasimi territories became part of the Trucial States under British protection. The family continued to rule their respective emirates through the colonial period and into the formation of the United Arab Emirates in 1971, with Ras Al Khaimah joining in 1972.
data.description: The Al Qasimi is a Hashemite Arab dynasty that rules Sharjah and Ras Al Khaimah in the UAE. They are descended from Husayn ibn Ali and were historically a powerful maritime confederation that controlled territories on both sides of the Persian Gulf.
data.originStory: The Al Qasimi trace their lineage to Hashemite origins, descending from Husayn ibn Ali through their eponymous ancestor Al Qasim bin Idris bin Ja'far al-Zaki. The Qawasim tribe from which the dynasty originates are Huwala, with their ancestors migrating and maintaining connections between the Arabian Peninsula and Persia, particularly maintaining rulership over the town of Lengeh on the coast of Hormozgan province until 1887.
data.modernStatus: Currently ruling family of Sharjah under Sultan bin Muhammad Al-Qasimi and Ras Al Khaimah under Saud bin Saqr Al Qasimi. Both emirates are constituent members of the United Arab Emirates federation established in 1971-1972.
data.nameEtymology: Named after their eponymous ancestor Al Qasim bin Idris bin Ja'far al-Zaki

migrationPath.description: Huwala migration and establishment between Arabian Peninsula and Persian coasts

data.history: Based on the research provided, there is no specific historical information about Umm Suqeim families as a distinct genealogical group. The research mentions Umm Suqeim only as a geographic location in Dubai, referenced in archaeological contexts (Al Sufouh and Umm Suqeim Beach), and as the location of a cat café that opened in 2018. The broader historical context shows Dubai was established in the early 18th century as a pearling and fishing settlement, populated by members of the Bani Yas tribe until 1833, when the Al Bu Falasah tribe seceded from Abu Dhabi and established themselves in Dubai under the leadership of Obeid bin Saeed and Maktoum bin Butti. However, no specific information is provided about families specifically identified with the Umm Suqeim area or their origins, tribal affiliations, or genealogical connections.
data.description: No specific information about Umm Suqeim families as a genealogical entity is provided in the research; only geographic references to the Umm Suqeim area of Dubai are mentioned.

data.history: Emirates Hills is a gated community in Dubai, United Arab Emirates, developed by Emaar Properties as a freehold residential area open for purchase by anyone. The development was named after Beverly Hills and has been referred to as 'Dubai's old-money enclave' and the 'crown jewel for high-net-worth investors.' The community is notable for its large plot sizes, location next to two golf courses (Montgomerie and Emirates Golf Club), lake-facing mansions, and central location. The majority of owners are from wealthy families in the region, making it one of the most exclusive neighborhoods in the GCC. Properties look out over the fairways of the Address Montgomerie golf course, created by architect Desmond Muirhead and golfer Colin Montgomerie.
data.description: Emirates Hills is a luxury gated community in Dubai known as 'Dubai's old-money enclave' and home to some of the wealthiest families in the GCC region. The community features large plots, golf course views, and lake-facing mansions.
data.modernStatus: Emirates Hills continues to attract ultra-high-net-worth individuals with recent sales including Lakshmi Mittal's AED 425 million villa purchase in 2025 and Petra Ecclestone's $70 million home purchase in 2026. Property values have reached record levels with some villas selling for AED 18,489 per square foot.
data.nameEtymology: Named after Beverly Hills

data.history: The research provided does not contain any information about 'Arabian Ranches families' as a genealogical group or tribal entity. The only reference to Arabian Ranches appears in the context of Jumeirah English Speaking School (JESS), which established a campus at Arabian Ranches in September 2005 as part of a planned residential development in Dubai. This was the first school in Dubai to be built as part of such a development, opening with 573 students and initially offering primary education and Years 7 and 8. The Arabian Ranches campus expanded over time, adding a sixth form centre in 2009 and launching the IB Diploma Programme. However, no information is provided about specific families, their origins, tribal affiliations, or genealogical connections to this location.

data.history: The Al Hindawi family traces its origins to Palestine, where they lived as a wealthy and influential family before 1948. Following the declaration of Israeli independence and the Nakba in 1948, the family fled from Palestine to Jordan, settling in the Naharayim region. The family maintained their prominent status in Jordan, with two of Nezar al-Hindawi's uncles serving as members of the Jordanian government, indicating their continued political influence in their new homeland. The family was forced to relocate again after the Six-Day War in 1967 when their village was destroyed by Israeli Defense Forces. This second displacement, along with the events of Black September in Jordan in 1970, had a radicalizing effect on family members, particularly Nezar al-Hindawi, who turned against the Jordanian government and became involved in political activism and later terrorism. By the late 1970s, some family members had dispersed internationally, with Nezar's father and brother living in London, while an estimated 500 family members were reported to be living in Syria by the 1980s, where they became vulnerable to Syrian intelligence pressure during Nezar's involvement in the 1986 Hindawi Affair.
data.description: A Palestinian family that fled to Jordan in 1948, known for their wealth and political influence, with members scattered across Jordan, Syria, and London by the 1980s.
data.originStory: The Al Hindawi family originated in Palestine, where they were established as a wealthy and influential family before being forced to flee during the 1948 Nakba following Israeli independence.
data.modernStatus: The family's modern status is unclear following the notoriety of the 1986 Hindawi Affair, though they maintained political connections in Jordan through the 1980s with family members serving in government positions.

data.history: The Jishi family appears to be an established Bahraini family with notable contributions to civil service, human rights activism, and public service. The research reveals members active in various professional fields including diplomacy, media, and human rights work. Bahia Jawad Al Jishi represents the family's diplomatic achievements, having served in various government positions since the 1970s, including as a newscaster and children's program supervisor, before being appointed to the Consultative Council in 2000 as one of its first female members. She later became Bahrain's ambassador to Belgium, Luxembourg, and Denmark, while also representing the country at the EU and NATO. The family has legal connections as well, with Mohammed al-Jishi serving as a lawyer for prominent human rights activists including Abdulhadi al-Khawaja and Nabeel Rajab during significant political cases in the 2010s.
data.description: The Jishi family is a prominent Bahraini family known for their contributions to diplomacy, civil service, and legal advocacy, with members serving in high-level government positions and representing human rights activists.
data.modernStatus: The family maintains prominent positions in Bahraini society, with members in diplomatic service, legal profession, and civil service roles, representing Bahrain internationally and domestically in various capacities.

data.history: The Khouri surname (also transliterated as Khoury) derives from the Arabic خوري (Khūrī), meaning 'priest' in Levantine Arabic, originating from the Latin word curia or French curé meaning parish priest. Historically, the name was given to new priests or ministers in Eastern churches, replacing their previous surnames, and was passed down to their children and descendants. The various Eastern churches, including Greek Orthodox, Maronite, Melkite Catholic, and Syriac Orthodox, allowed married priests, enabling the surname to be transmitted through generations. The Khouri family has spread throughout the Levant region (Syria, Lebanon, Jordan, Palestine) and has produced numerous notable figures across business, politics, academia, and the arts. In the modern era, many Khouri family members have achieved international prominence, including Academy Award winner Callie Khouri, business leaders like Elie Khouri who founded Vivium and serves as chairman of Omnicom Media Group MENA, and various politicians and academics who have contributed significantly to their respective fields.
data.description: The Khouri family is a prominent Levantine Christian family whose surname means 'priest' in Arabic, historically associated with Eastern Christian clergy across Syria, Lebanon, Jordan, and Palestine.
data.originStory: The surname originated from the practice of giving the title 'Khouri' (priest) to new clergy members in Eastern Christian churches, who would adopt it as their family name, replacing their previous surname. This name was then passed to their children and descendants, as Eastern churches allowed married priesthood.
data.modernStatus: The Khouri family maintains significant influence in business, politics, academia, and arts across the Levant and internationally, with members holding leadership positions in major corporations, government ministries, and cultural institutions.
data.nameEtymology: Derives from Arabic خوري (Khūrī) meaning 'priest' in Levantine Arabic, from Latin curia or French curé meaning parish priest, ultimately from Latin curatus meaning 'one responsible for the care of souls'

data.history: The research provides limited information about the Abdullatif family itself, but reveals key details through the marriage connection between the Al Sheikh and Al Saud families. Tarfa bint Abdullah Al Sheikh's father was Abdullah bin Abdullatif Al Sheikh, a member of the prominent Al Sheikh religious family who served as one of the principal religious teachers and advisers to Emir Abdulaziz bin Abdul Rahman. However, Abdullah was initially a supporter of Emir Muhammad bin Abdullah Al Rashid until Abdulaziz captured Riyadh in 1902. The marriage of Tarfa to Emir Abdulaziz immediately after the capture of Riyadh in 1902 was part of strategic alliances to strengthen links between the Al Saud and Al Sheikh families. Tarfa's sisters also married into the Al Saud family - Munira married Abdulaziz's half-brother Muhammad bin Abdul Rahman, and Sara married his full-brother Saad bin Abdul Rahman. The family held significant religious authority in Nejd, with connections to Muhammad ibn Abd al-Wahhab as eighth generation descendants. While the research mentions other individuals named Abdullatif in different contexts (including a Sudanese nationalist and a 2022 shrine attacker), these appear unrelated to the Gulf Abdullatif family.
data.description: A family connected to the prominent Al Sheikh religious lineage in Nejd, with Abdullah bin Abdullatif Al Sheikh serving as a religious teacher and adviser to the Saudi leadership in the early 20th century.

data.history: The Al Jufairi (also spelled Al Juffairi) is a highly prominent family in Qatar, with historical significance dating back several centuries. The family was the most prevalent tribe in the Barahat Al Jufairi area of Doha at the time of the district's naming, which was used as a central marketplace historically. The settlement was specifically named after a member of the Al Jufairi family, demonstrating their prominent status in early Qatar. In contemporary times, the family has produced notable figures including Mohammed Issa Altajer, a prominent Bahraini human rights lawyer whose wife Huda al-Juffairi is a medical doctor who provided aid to protesters during the 2011 Arab Spring in Bahrain. The family has also been represented in Qatar's political institutions, with Sheikha bint Yousuf Al-Jufairi serving as a member of Qatar's Consultative Assembly, where she was re-elected in 2015 and chaired the Legal Committee, advocating for amendments to expand the council's functions.
data.description: A highly prominent Qatari family with historical significance in Doha, known for their influence in the Barahat Al Jufairi district and contemporary involvement in law, medicine, and politics across the Gulf region.
data.modernStatus: The Al Jufairi family maintains prominence in Qatar and the broader Gulf region, with members active in law, medicine, and politics. They have representation in Qatar's Consultative Assembly and continue to be associated with the historic Barahat Al Jufairi district of Doha.
data.nameEtymology: The family name Al Jufairi (الجفيري) appears to be derived from Arabic roots, though the specific etymology is not detailed in the available research.
